title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
2.1 Model Definition
The Erdős–Rényi model \(G(n, p)\) (Gilbert 1959; Erdős & Rényi 1959–60) is the simplest possible random graph: each of the \(\binom{n}{2}\) potential edges exists independently with probability \(p\).
The adjacency matrix entries are i.i.d. Bernoulli:
\[A_{ij} = A_{ji} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p) \quad \forall i < j\]
The probability of drawing a particular graph with \(m\) edges is:
\[\Pr(G) = p^m (1-p)^{\binom{n}{2} - m}\]
The G(n, m) variant fixes the number of edges at exactly \(m\), choosing uniformly among all graphs with that edge count. As we will see (PS1 Q3), the two models are equivalent for large \(n\) when \(m = \langle m \rangle\).

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
2.2 Expected Properties
Expected degree: each node can link to \(n-1\) others, each with probability \(p\): \[\langle k \rangle = (n-1)p\]
Expected edges: \(\langle m \rangle = \binom{n}{2} p = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} p\)
To study “interesting” limiting behaviour we want \(\langle k \rangle = c\) fixed as \(n \to \infty\). This requires \(p = c/(n-1) \to 0\), so the graph becomes sparser as it grows.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
section: 2.3 Degree Distribution: From Binomial to Poisson
text:
2.3 Degree Distribution: From Binomial to Poisson
Each node independently connects to each of the other \(n-1\) nodes with probability \(p\), so degree follows a Binomial:
\[\Pr(k) = \binom{n-1}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-1-k}\]
For \(p = c/(n-1)\) and \(n \to \infty\), this converges to a Poisson distribution:
\[\Pr(k) \approx \frac{c^k}{k!} e^{-c}\]
The derivation uses \(\binom{n-1}{k} p^k \approx c^k/k!\) and \((1-p)^{n-1-k} \approx e^{-c}\) for large \(n\). Both mean and variance of this Poisson are equal to \(c\).
Why this matters (and fails): the Poisson distribution has exponentially decaying tails. The probability of a node having degree \(10c\) drops like \((10c)^{10c} e^{-10c} / (10c)!\), which is vanishingly small. Real networks have fat tails: hubs with degree far exceeding the mean are common, not exponentially rare. We will return to this when discussing power laws.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
2.4 Clustering: A Critical Failure
The global clustering coefficient in \(G(n,p)\) is simply:
\[C = \frac{\text{triangles} \times 3}{\text{connected triples}} \approx \frac{\binom{n}{3} p^3}{\binom{n}{3} p^2} = p = \frac{c}{n-1}\]
As \(n \to \infty\) with \(c\) fixed: \(C \to 0\). ER graphs are locally tree-like: there are essentially no triangles, and no cycles of any finite length, in the large-\(n\) limit.
This is disqualifying as a model for social networks. Real friendship networks have \(C\) in the range 0.1–0.5, while ER predicts \(C \sim 10^{-3}\) for the same parameters. The “friend of my friend is also my friend” structure of human social life is absent by construction in ER.


Code
set.seed(42)
c_target <- 5.0

## (A) Degree distribution: simulated vs Poisson theory
par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 4, 1))

# Overlapping histograms for n = 100, 500, 2000
all_n     <- c(100, 500, 2000)
all_cols  <- c("#aec7e8", "#1f77b4", "#084594")
k_range   <- 0:19

# Build density matrices for plotting
plot(NULL, xlim = c(0, 19), ylim = c(0, 0.25),
     xlab = "Degree k", ylab = "P(k)",
     main = "Degree → Poisson as n → ∞")
for (i in seq_along(all_n)) {
  n   <- all_n[i]
  p   <- c_target / (n - 1)
  g_t <- erdos.renyi.game(n = n, p = p, type = "gnp")
  d   <- degree(g_t)
  # Use hist with add=TRUE trick via density
  h <- hist(d, breaks = -1:20 + 0.5, plot = FALSE)
  rect(h$breaks[-length(h$breaks)], 0, h$breaks[-1], h$density,
       col = adjustcolor(all_cols[i], alpha.f = 0.4), border = NA)
}
lines(k_range, dpois(k_range, lambda = c_target),
      col = "red", lwd = 2, type = "b", pch = 19, cex = 0.6)
legend("topright", legend = c("n=100", "n=500", "n=2000",
                               paste0("Poisson(c=", c_target, ")")),
       fill   = c(adjustcolor(all_cols, 0.5), NA),
       lty    = c(NA, NA, NA, 1), col = c(NA, NA, NA, "red"),
       border = c(all_cols, NA), bty = "n", cex = 0.8)

## (B) Clustering vs n (log-y scale)
ns      <- c(50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000)
c_vals  <- c(3, 5, 10)
col_b   <- c("#4C72B0", "#DD8452", "#55A868")

plot(NULL, xlim = range(ns), ylim = c(1e-4, 0.4),
     log  = "y",
     xlab = "n", ylab = "C(A)  [log scale]",
     main = "Clustering C → 0 as n → ∞\n(dashed = theory p = c/(n-1))")
for (j in seq_along(c_vals)) {
  c_v      <- c_vals[j]
  obs_C    <- numeric(length(ns))
  er_pred  <- numeric(length(ns))
  for (i in seq_along(ns)) {
    n <- ns[i]
    p <- c_v / (n - 1)
    g_t      <- erdos.renyi.game(n = n, p = p, type = "gnp")
    obs_C[i] <- transitivity(g_t, type = "global")
    er_pred[i] <- p
  }
  lines(ns, obs_C,   col = col_b[j], lwd = 2, type = "b", pch = 19, cex = 0.5)
  lines(ns, er_pred, col = col_b[j], lwd = 1.5, lty = 2)
}
legend("topright", legend = paste0("c=", c_vals, " (sim)"),
       col = col_b, lwd = 2, bty = "n", cex = 0.8)

title: 02; Random Graph Models
section: 2.5 Phase Transitions and the Giant Component
text:
2.5 Phase Transitions and the Giant Component

2.5.1 The Phase Transition Phenomenon
Perhaps the most beautiful result in random graph theory is the sudden emergence of a giant component; a connected component containing a finite fraction of all nodes; as the average degree \(c\) crosses 1.






ImportantErdős–Rényi Phase Transition



At \(c = \langle k \rangle = 1\), there is a sharp phase transition:

Subcritical (\(c < 1\)): all components have size \(O(\log n)\). The largest component as a fraction of \(n\) vanishes.
Supercritical (\(c > 1\)): a giant component of size \(\sim Sn\) exists, where \(S > 0\) solves \(S = 1 - e^{-cS}\).
Critical (\(c = 1\)): largest component is \(O(n^{2/3})\); diverging, but sub-linear in \(n\).





2.5.2 Deriving the Giant Component Equation
Let \(u\) be the probability that a randomly chosen node is not in the giant component. Node \(i\) is not in the GC if, for every other node \(j\), either there is no edge \((i,j)\) (probability \(1-p\)) or there is an edge but \(j\) is not in the GC (probability \(pu\)). Since edges are independent:
\[u = (1 - p + pu)^{n-1}\]
Substituting \(p = c/(n-1)\) and taking logs:
\[\ln u = (n-1) \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{c(1-u)}{n-1}\right) \approx -(n-1) \cdot \frac{c(1-u)}{n-1} = -c(1-u)\]
Letting \(S = 1 - u\) (the fraction in the GC):
\[\boxed{S = 1 - e^{-cS}}\]
This transcendental equation has no closed-form solution, but we can solve it graphically or numerically. For \(c \leq 1\): the only solution is \(S = 0\). For \(c > 1\): a unique positive solution \(S \in (0, 1)\) exists.


2.5.3 Full Connectivity
Once the giant component absorbs almost all nodes, we can ask: when does the entire network become connected (no isolated nodes)?
An isolated node survives if all \(n-1\) potential neighbours are absent. The expected number of isolated nodes is:
\[I_n = n(1-p)^{n-1} \approx n e^{-np} \approx n e^{-c}\]
For the last isolated node to disappear (\(I_n = 1\)), we need \(p \sim \ln(n)/n\), corresponding to \(c = \langle k \rangle \sim \ln n\).
Summary of phase transitions:



\(p\) (order)
Event




\(1/n^2\)
First edge appears


\(1/n^{3/2}\)
First triple (path of length 2)


\(1/n\)
Giant component emerges (\(c = 1\))


\(\ln n / n\)
Graph becomes fully connected





Code
## Theoretical S via bisection
gc_theory <- function(c) {
  if (c <= 1) return(0.0)
  f <- function(S) S - (1 - exp(-c * S))
  uniroot(f, c(1e-9, 1 - 1e-9))$root
}

set.seed(42)
n       <- 1000
c_range <- seq(0, 4, length.out = 80)

gc_frac_sim <- sapply(c_range, function(c) {
  p     <- c / (n - 1)
  g_tmp <- erdos.renyi.game(n = n, p = p, type = "gnp")
  max(components(g_tmp)$csize) / n
})

S_theory <- sapply(c_range, gc_theory)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 4, 1))

## (A) Simulated vs theory
plot(c_range, gc_frac_sim,
     pch = 19, cex = 0.5, col = adjustcolor("#4C72B0", 0.6),
     xlab = expression(paste("Average degree  ", c == langle*k*rangle)),
     ylab = "Giant component size / n",
     main = expression(paste("Phase Transition in ", G(n, p))),
     ylim = c(-0.02, 1.02))
lines(c_range, S_theory, col = "red", lwd = 2)
abline(v = 1, lty = 2, lwd = 1.5, col = "black")
legend("topleft",
       legend = c(paste0("Simulated (n=", n, ")"),
                  expression(paste("Theory: ", S == 1 - e^{-cS})),
                  "Critical point c=1"),
       col    = c(adjustcolor("#4C72B0", 0.6), "red", "black"),
       pch    = c(19, NA, NA), lty = c(NA, 1, 2), lwd = c(NA, 2, 1.5),
       bty    = "n", cex = 0.85)

## (B) Fixed-point geometry
S_vals <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 200)
c_fp   <- c(0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0)
fp_cols <- c("#aec7e8", "#DD8452", "#C44E52", "#8B0000")
fp_labs <- c("c=0.5 (sub)", "c=1.0 (critical)", "c=2.0 (super)", "c=3.0 (super)")

plot(NULL, xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1),
     xlab = "S", ylab = expression(1 - e^{-cS}),
     main = expression(paste("Fixed-point: ", S == 1 - e^{-cS},
                             "\n(intersection = GC fraction)")))
for (j in seq_along(c_fp)) {
  lines(S_vals, 1 - exp(-c_fp[j] * S_vals),
        col = fp_cols[j], lwd = 1.8)
}
abline(0, 1, lty = 2, lwd = 1.5)
legend("topleft", legend = c(fp_labs, "S (identity)"),
       col = c(fp_cols, "black"), lwd = 2,
       lty = c(rep(1, 4), 2), bty = "n", cex = 0.85)





Phase transition in G(n,p). The giant component jumps from 0 to O(n) at c=1.




Code
## Print GC fractions at key c values
for (c_v in c(0.5, 0.9, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0)) {
  S <- gc_theory(c_v)
  diam_log <- log(1000) / log(max(c_v, 1.001))
  cat(sprintf("c = %.1f:  theory S = %.4f,  diameter ≈ ln(n)/ln(c) = %.1f\n",
              c_v, S, diam_log))
}

c = 0.9:  theory S = 0.0000,  diameter ≈ ln(n)/ln(c) = 6911.2
c = 1.0:  theory S = 0.0000,  diameter ≈ ln(n)/ln(c) = 6911.2
c = 1.5:  theory S = 0.5828,  diameter ≈ ln(n)/ln(c) = 17.0
c = 2.0:  theory S = 0.7968,  diameter ≈ ln(n)/ln(c) = 10.0
c = 3.0:  theory S = 0.9405,  diameter ≈ ln(n)/ln(c) = 6.3




2.5.4 Contagion on ER Graphs (Application)
The phase transition directly models epidemic thresholds. Consider an SI (susceptible-infected) model on \(G(n,p)\):

A fraction \(\pi\) of nodes are vaccinated (immune)
Effective graph: \(G((1-\pi)n, p)\); remove immune nodes
The disease spreads iff this effective graph has a giant component

The epidemic threshold is when the effective average degree exceeds 1: \(c_{\text{eff}} = (1-\pi) c > 1\), i.e., \(\pi < 1 - 1/c\).
This is the herd immunity threshold in network terms. For the network to be protected, we must vaccinate at least fraction \(1 - 1/c\) of nodes.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
section: 3.1 Why We Need More Than ER
text:
3.1 Why We Need More Than ER
Erdős–Rényi produces Poisson degree distributions. Real networks have heavy-tailed degree distributions; power laws, log-normals. The configuration model decouples the degree sequence from the wiring pattern: we can input any degree sequence \(\{k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n\}\) and get a “random” graph that has those exact degrees.
The construction (Bender & Canfield 1978):

Give node \(i\) exactly \(k_i\) stubs (half-edges)
Choose a uniformly random perfect matching of all \(\sum_i k_i\) stubs
Each matched pair of stubs becomes an edge

This produces multi-edges and self-loops with probability \(O(1/n)\), which vanishes in large networks. For our purposes, we treat the resulting graph as simple.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
3.2 Key Properties
Linking probability between nodes \(i\) and \(j\): \[p_{ij} = \frac{k_i k_j}{\sum_l k_l} = \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}\]
This is a heterogeneous ER model: high-degree nodes are more likely to connect to each other; and to everyone else.
Expected number of common neighbours between \(i\) and \(j\): \[N_{ij} = \sum_l p_{il} p_{lj} = \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \cdot \frac{\langle k^2 \rangle - \langle k \rangle}{\langle k \rangle}\]
Clustering still vanishes: \(C = O(1/n)\) for fixed \(\langle k \rangle\), just as in ER. The configuration model fixes the degree sequence but still wires edges randomly; no triangle-formation mechanism exists.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
3.3 The Friendship Paradox
One of the most surprising properties of configuration models:






ImportantFriendship Paradox (Feld 1991)



On average, your friends have more friends than you do.
Formally, the expected degree of a uniformly chosen neighbour is:
\[\langle k_{\text{nbr}} \rangle = \frac{\langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle} = \langle k \rangle + \frac{\text{Var}(k)}{\langle k \rangle} \geq \langle k \rangle\]
with equality only when all degrees are equal.


Why: when you follow a random edge, the probability of landing at node \(j\) is proportional to \(k_j\) (high-degree nodes contribute more stubs, so are more likely to be at the other end of a randomly chosen edge). This size-biased sampling over-represents hubs.
Economic consequences (Jackson 2019, JPE): the friendship paradox creates systematic misperceptions. If your friends are on average richer, healthier, or better-connected than you, you may mis-calibrate social norms and make suboptimal choices. Jackson shows this can cascade into information avalanches: even though only 1/3 of the population genuinely prefers choice A, biased sampling through social networks can make it look like the unanimous favourite.
Giant component in the configuration model: emerges when \(\langle k^2 \rangle - 2\langle k \rangle > 0\). For a Poisson distribution (\(\langle k^2 \rangle = \langle k \rangle^2 + \langle k \rangle\)), this reduces to \(\langle k \rangle > 1\); recovering the ER result. For heavy-tailed distributions, the condition is weaker: networks with power-law degree distributions essentially always have a giant component.


Code
## Load American75 (re-use g_fb from earlier chunk, or reload)
mat      <- readMat("American75.mat")
A_sp     <- mat$A
A_t      <- as(A_sp, "TsparseMatrix")
edges_fb <- cbind(A_t@i + 1L, A_t@j + 1L)
g_real   <- graph_from_edgelist(edges_fb, directed = FALSE)
g_real   <- simplify(g_real)

degrees  <- degree(g_real)
n_nodes  <- vcount(g_real)

# Mean degree of each node's neighbourhood
nbr_mean_k <- sapply(seq_len(n_nodes), function(v) {
  nbrs <- neighbors(g_real, v)
  if (length(nbrs) == 0) return(0.0)
  mean(degrees[nbrs])
})

mean_k    <- mean(degrees)
mean_k2   <- mean(degrees^2)
theory_nbr <- mean_k2 / mean_k

mask <- degrees >= 2

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 4, 1))

## (A) Scatter: own degree vs mean neighbour degree
plot(degrees[mask], nbr_mean_k[mask],
     pch = 19, cex = 0.4, col = adjustcolor("#4C72B0", 0.3),
     xlab = expression(paste("Own degree  ", k[i])),
     ylab = "Mean neighbour degree",
     main = "Friendship Paradox\n(points above red line = paradox)")
abline(0, 1, col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 1.5)
legend("topleft", legend = "Equal line", col = "red", lty = 2, bty = "n")

## (B) Distribution comparison
max_deg <- max(degrees)
h_own <- hist(degrees,           breaks = seq(-0.5, max_deg + 0.5, 1),
              plot = FALSE)
h_nbr <- hist(nbr_mean_k[mask],  breaks = seq(0, max(nbr_mean_k[mask]) + 5, 5),
              plot = FALSE)

plot(h_own$mids, h_own$density, type = "s", col = "#4C72B0", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Degree", ylab = "Density",
     main = "Degree distributions",
     xlim = c(0, quantile(nbr_mean_k[mask], 0.99)),
     ylim = c(0, max(h_own$density) * 1.1))
lines(h_nbr$mids, h_nbr$density, type = "s", col = "#C44E52", lwd = 2)
abline(v = mean_k,               col = "#4C72B0", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
abline(v = mean(nbr_mean_k[mask]),col = "#C44E52", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
abline(v = theory_nbr,            col = "black",   lty = 3, lwd = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = c(sprintf("Own degree (mean=%.1f)", mean_k),
                  sprintf("Nbr degree (mean=%.1f)", mean(nbr_mean_k[mask])),
                  sprintf("Theory <k²>/<k> = %.1f", theory_nbr)),
       col = c("#4C72B0", "#C44E52", "black"),
       lwd = 2, lty = c(1, 1, 3), bty = "n", cex = 0.85)

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
4.1 The Core Tension
We have two stylised facts about real social networks:

High clustering: people in the same community know each other (\(C \sim 0.1\)–\(0.5\)).
Short average path lengths: “six degrees of separation” suggests \(\langle \ell \rangle = O(\log n)\).

These seem contradictory. High clustering requires lots of local triangles; which means most of your edges connect to people already in your cluster. Short paths require long-range connections that skip across the network. How can both hold simultaneously?
Erdős–Rényi achieves short paths (random wiring is great for connectivity) but kills clustering. Can we do better?

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
4.2 The Model
Watts & Strogatz (1998) propose a one-parameter interpolation:

Start with a regular ring lattice: \(n\) nodes arranged in a circle, each connected to its \(k\) nearest neighbours (so each node has degree \(k\)). This has high clustering and \(O(n/k)\) diameter.
Rewire each edge independently with probability \(\beta\): one endpoint is kept, the other is replaced with a uniformly random node.

At \(\beta = 0\): original lattice; high \(C\), large \(\langle \ell \rangle\). At \(\beta = 1\): Erdős–Rényi; low \(C\), small \(\langle \ell \rangle\).
The key insight: a small number of rewirings (small \(\beta > 0\)) dramatically collapses path lengths while leaving clustering nearly intact. This is because one “shortcut” across the network can reduce distances between two entire clusters from \(O(n)\) to \(O(1)\).
Clustering of the lattice (exact formula for even \(k\)): \[C_{\text{lattice}} = \frac{3(k-2)}{4(k-1)} \approx \frac{3}{4} \quad (k \gg 1)\]


Code
set.seed(42)
n_ws  <- 200
k_ws  <- 6   # each node connects to k_ws nearest neighbours

# Baseline lattice (beta = 0)
g0 <- sample_smallworld(dim = 1, size = n_ws, nei = k_ws / 2, p = 0)
C0 <- transitivity(g0, type = "global")
L0 <- mean_distance(g0, directed = FALSE)

betas  <- 10^seq(-3, 0, length.out = 30)
C_norm <- numeric(length(betas))
L_norm <- numeric(length(betas))

for (i in seq_along(betas)) {
  g_ws      <- sample_smallworld(dim = 1, size = n_ws, nei = k_ws / 2, p = betas[i])
  C_norm[i] <- transitivity(g_ws, type = "global") / C0
  L_norm[i] <- mean_distance(g_ws, directed = FALSE) / L0
}

par(mar = c(4, 4, 4, 1))
plot(betas, C_norm,
     log  = "x", type = "b", pch = 19, cex = 0.6,
     col  = "#4C72B0", lwd = 2,
     xlab = expression(paste("Rewiring probability  ", beta)),
     ylab = "Normalised value",
     main = sprintf("Watts–Strogatz Small World\n(n=%d, k=%d)", n_ws, k_ws),
     ylim = c(0, 1.05))
lines(betas, L_norm, type = "b", pch = 15, cex = 0.6,
      col = "#C44E52", lwd = 2)
# Shade small-world regime
rect(1e-2, 0, 0.1, 1.05,
     col = adjustcolor("green", alpha.f = 0.12), border = NA)
legend("left",
       legend = c(expression(C(beta)/C(0)~~"(clustering)"),
                  expression(langle*l*rangle(beta)/langle*l*rangle(0)~~"(avg path length)"),
                  "Small-world regime"),
       col    = c("#4C72B0", "#C44E52", adjustcolor("green", 0.3)),
       lwd    = c(2, 2, 8), pch = c(19, 15, NA), bty = "n", cex = 0.85)
grid()





Watts–Strogatz: clustering stays high while diameter collapses for small β.




Code
cat(sprintf("At β = 0 (lattice): C = %.3f  L = %.2f\n", C0, L0))


At β = 0 (lattice): C = 0.600  L = 17.09

cat(sprintf("At β ≈ %.4f: L drops to 50%% of L0, C still at %.0f%% of C0\n",
            betas[idx_half_L], 100 * C_norm[idx_half_L]))


At β ≈ 0.0053: L drops to 50% of L0, C still at 95% of C0

                    m = n_ws * k_ws / 2)
cat(sprintf("At β = 1 (ER approx): C = %.3f  L = %.2f\n",
            transitivity(g_rnd, type = "global"),
            mean_distance(g_rnd, directed = FALSE)))

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
4.3 Limitations of Watts–Strogatz
While WS solves the clustering–diameter tension, it still produces a Poisson-like degree distribution (the rewiring changes some degrees slightly but does not create hubs). Real networks have heavy tails. This failure motivates the next model.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
5.1 The Empirical Pattern
Across an enormous range of systems; citations, hyperlinks, phone calls, metabolic networks; the degree distribution follows a power law:
\[p(k) \propto k^{-\gamma}\]
On a log-log plot, this appears as a straight line with slope \(-\gamma\). Empirical exponents typically fall in the range \(\gamma \in (2, 3)\).
The power law is qualitatively different from the Poisson: the standard deviation scales with the mean (\(\sigma \sim \langle k \rangle\) for \(\gamma \approx 3\), and even \(\sigma = \infty\) for \(\gamma \leq 3\)). There is no “typical” degree; the distribution is scale-free.






NoteWhy “scale-free”?



A function \(f(x) = x^{-\gamma}\) satisfies \(f(cx) = c^{-\gamma} f(x)\): scaling the argument by a constant changes the function by a constant factor, regardless of the starting value. There is no characteristic scale.
For the Poisson with mean \(c\): the probability of degree \(5c\) vs \(c\) drops by a factor \(e^{-4c}\), which depends strongly on \(c\). The Poisson has a scale (namely, \(c\)). The power law does not.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
5.2 The Barabási–Albert Model: Preferential Attachment
Barabási & Albert (1999) proposed a simple growing network mechanism that generates power-law degree distributions:

Start with \(m_0\) nodes.
At each timestep \(t\), add one new node with \(m\) edges.
Each new edge connects to existing node \(i\) with probability proportional to \(k_i(t)\) (“rich get richer”): \[\Pr(\text{new edge} \to i) = \frac{k_i(t)}{\sum_j k_j(t)} = \frac{k_i(t)}{2t}\]


5.2.1 Mean-Field Analysis
Track the expected degree of node \(i\) as a continuous-time ODE. Node \(i\) gains one edge per timestep with probability \(k_i/(2t)\):
\[\frac{d k_i}{dt} = m \cdot \frac{k_i(t)}{2t}\]
This is a separable ODE. Separating variables: \(dk_i / k_i = m \, dt / (2t)\), integrating:
\[k_i(t) = m \left(\frac{t}{i}\right)^{1/2}\]
where \(i\) is the time node \(i\) was born (boundary condition \(k_i(i) = m\)). Older nodes accumulate more links; this is the first-mover advantage demonstrated empirically in citation networks (Newman’s papers, PS1 Q4).


5.2.2 Deriving the Power Law
From \(k_i(t) = m\sqrt{t/i}\), the fraction of nodes with degree \(\leq k\) at time \(t\) is the fraction of nodes born after time \(t \cdot (m/k)^2\):
\[F_t(k) = \Pr(k_i(t) \leq k) = \Pr\!\left(i \geq \frac{m^2 t}{k^2}\right) = 1 - \frac{m^2 t / k^2}{t} = 1 - \frac{m^2}{k^2}\]
The density:
\[f(k) = \frac{dF}{dk} = \frac{2m^2}{k^3} \propto k^{-3}\]
The Barabási–Albert model always produces \(\gamma = 3\), regardless of \(m\). This is a specific prediction. Empirically \(\gamma\) varies, which motivates hybrid models.


5.2.3 Maximum Hub Size
For a network of \(n\) nodes with power-law exponent \(\gamma\), the maximum degree scales as:
\[k_{\max} \sim k_{\min} \cdot n^{1/(\gamma-1)}\]
For \(\gamma = 3\): \(k_{\max} \sim \sqrt{n}\). For the WWW with \(n \sim 10^9\): \(k_{\max} \sim 30{,}000\); consistent with observed super-hubs.


Code
set.seed(42)
par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 4, 1))

## (A) Degree CCDF of BA model
g_ba    <- sample_pa(n = 5000, m = 3, directed = FALSE)
degs_ba <- degree(g_ba)
ks_sorted <- sort(unique(degs_ba))
ccdf_ba   <- sapply(ks_sorted, function(k) mean(degs_ba >= k))

plot(ks_sorted, ccdf_ba,
     log  = "xy", pch = 19, cex = 0.4,
     col  = adjustcolor("#4C72B0", 0.7),
     xlab = "Degree k", ylab = expression(bar(F)(k)~~"(CCDF)"),
     main = "BA Model: Power-Law CCDF")
# OLS fit on log-log scale for k in [5, 200]
mask_fit  <- ks_sorted >= 5 & ks_sorted <= 200
lm_fit    <- lm(log(ccdf_ba[mask_fit]) ~ log(ks_sorted[mask_fit]))
slope_fit <- coef(lm_fit)[2]
k_line    <- ks_sorted[mask_fit]
lines(k_line, exp(coef(lm_fit)[1]) * k_line^slope_fit,
      col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("bottomleft",
       legend = c("BA model",
                  sprintf("Fit slope = %.2f\n(theory: -2)", slope_fit)),
       col    = c(adjustcolor("#4C72B0", 0.7), "red"),
       pch    = c(19, NA), lty = c(NA, 2), lwd = c(NA, 2),
       bty    = "n", cex = 0.85)

## (B) First-mover advantage
n_ba_fm  <- 2000
m_ba     <- 2
g_ba_fm  <- sample_pa(n = n_ba_fm, m = m_ba, directed = FALSE)
degs_fm  <- degree(g_ba_fm)
birth_times <- seq_len(n_ba_fm)

q25 <- quantile(birth_times, 0.25)
q75 <- quantile(birth_times, 0.75)
early <- degs_fm[birth_times <= q25]
late  <- degs_fm[birth_times >= q75]

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
5.3 The Jackson–Rogers Hybrid Model
The BA model generates power-law degree distributions but fails on:

Clustering: \(C \to 0\) as in ER (pure random attachment produces no triangles)
Degree assortativity: BA has neutral assortativity (no correlation between a node’s degree and its neighbours’ degrees)

Jackson & Rogers (2007, AER) propose a two-stage attachment rule:

Each new node randomly meets \(m_r\) existing nodes (uniform attachment)
Then explores \(m_n\) additional nodes by following the neighbours of the first \(m_r\) nodes (network search)

This generates both a (roughly) power-law degree distribution AND positive clustering (because node \(i\) connects to both node \(j\) and \(j\)’s neighbours, creating triangles). The ratio \(r = m_r/m_n\) controls the trade-off:

\(r \to \infty\) (all uniform): exponential distribution, zero clustering
\(r \to 0\) (all network search): power law, positive clustering
Intermediate \(r\): interpolates; fits many real networks well

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
6.1 From Descriptive to Generative
We have seen community detection as an algorithmic problem (Lecture 3). But we can also approach it from a modelling perspective: postulate that the network was generated by a block model, and infer the parameters.
This is the Stochastic Block Model (SBM), which generalises ER to allow heterogeneous linking probabilities between groups.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
6.2 Model Definition
Assign each node \(i\) to a group \(z_i \in \{1, \ldots, k\}\). Given the group assignments, edges are drawn independently:
\[A_{ij} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(M_{z_i, z_j})\]
where \(M\) is a \(k \times k\) symmetric mixing matrix of connection probabilities.
Special cases: - \(M_{uv} = p\) for all \(u, v\): recovers Erdős–Rényi - \(M_{uu} = p_{\text{in}}\), \(M_{uv} = p_{\text{out}}\) for \(u \neq v\): the planted partition model; the simplest model with community structure

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
6.3 The Planted Partition Model
Set \(p = (2c + \varepsilon)/(2n)\) and \(q = (2c - \varepsilon)/(2n)\). Here \(c\) is the average degree, and \(\varepsilon\) controls community sharpness:

\(\varepsilon = 0\): ER (no community signal)
\(\varepsilon \gg 0\): very distinct communities

Detectability threshold (Decelle et al. 2011): below the threshold \(\varepsilon > 2\sqrt{c}\), no algorithm can reliably detect communities; not because it’s computationally hard, but because the signal is statistically indistinguishable from noise. This is a phase transition in the informativeness of the data.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
6.4 MLE Inference for SBM
Given a network \(G\) and assuming \(k\) communities, find the group assignments \(z\) and mixing matrix \(M\) that maximise the likelihood. Since each pair \((i,j)\) generates a Bernoulli with parameter \(M_{z_i, z_j}\):
\[\ln \mathcal{L}(G \mid z, M) = \sum_{i < j} \left[ A_{ij} \ln M_{z_i z_j} + (1 - A_{ij}) \ln(1 - M_{z_i z_j}) \right]\]
Maximising over \(M\) for fixed \(z\): standard Binomial MLE gives
\[\hat{M}_{uv} = \frac{E_{uv}}{N_{uv}}\]
where \(E_{uv}\) = observed edges between groups \(u\) and \(v\), and \(N_{uv}\) = total possible edges between them.
Maximising over \(z\) is NP-hard in general, but greedy heuristics work well.


Code
# NMI helper using base R (no sklearn dependency)
nmi_r <- function(a, b) {
  # mutual information via contingency table
  tab    <- table(a, b)
  n_obs  <- sum(tab)
  p_xy   <- tab / n_obs
  p_x    <- rowSums(p_xy)
  p_y    <- colSums(p_xy)
  # H(X), H(Y), H(X,Y)
  H_x  <- -sum(p_x[p_x > 0] * log(p_x[p_x > 0]))
  H_y  <- -sum(p_y[p_y > 0] * log(p_y[p_y > 0]))
  H_xy <- -sum(p_xy[p_xy > 0] * log(p_xy[p_xy > 0]))
  MI   <- H_x + H_y - H_xy
  if ((H_x + H_y) == 0) return(0)
  2 * MI / (H_x + H_y)
}

set.seed(42)
n_sbm   <- 200
k_sbm   <- 2
c_sbm   <- 5.0
thresh  <- 2 * sqrt(c_sbm)
eps_vec <- c(0.0, thresh * 0.8, thresh * 2.0)

par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(1, 1, 4, 1))
true_labels <- c(rep(0L, n_sbm / 2), rep(1L, n_sbm / 2))

for (eps in eps_vec) {
  p_in  <- (2 * c_sbm + eps)      / (2 * n_sbm)
  p_out <- max(0, (2 * c_sbm - eps) / (2 * n_sbm))

  pref_mat <- matrix(c(p_in, p_out, p_out, p_in), nrow = 2)
  g_sbm    <- sample_sbm(n_sbm, pref = pref_mat,
                          block.sizes = rep(n_sbm / k_sbm, k_sbm))

  # Community detection via Louvain
  mem      <- membership(cluster_louvain(g_sbm))
  nmi_val  <- nmi_r(true_labels, mem)

  v_colors <- ifelse(true_labels == 0, "#4C72B0", "#C44E52")
  set.seed(1)
  lay <- layout_with_kk(g_sbm)
  plot(g_sbm,
       layout             = lay,
       vertex.color       = v_colors,
       vertex.size        = 5,
       vertex.label       = NA,
       edge.color         = "#cccccc",
       edge.width         = 0.5,
       main               = sprintf("ε=%.1f  (threshold=%.1f)\nNMI=%.3f",
                                    eps, thresh, nmi_val))
}
mtext("Planted Partition Model: Detectability\n(Blue/Red = true communities)",
      outer = TRUE, line = -2, font = 2, cex = 1)





Planted partition model: varying ε around the detectability threshold.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
6.5 Degree-Corrected SBM (DC-SBM)
A known failure of the basic SBM: when the degree distribution within communities is heterogeneous, the MLE tends to group high-degree nodes together rather than finding true communities.
The DC-SBM (Karrer & Newman 2011) adds per-node propensity parameters \(\gamma_i \geq 0\):
\[A_{ij} \sim \text{Poisson}(\gamma_i \gamma_j M_{z_i z_j})\]
The \(\gamma_i\) absorbs degree heterogeneity, allowing the block structure \(M\) to capture community membership without being confounded by degree. MLE gives \(\hat{\gamma}_i = k_i / \kappa_{z_i}\) (node \(i\)’s degree as a fraction of its group’s total degree) and \(\hat{M}_{uv}\) as before.

title: 02; Random Graph Models
text:
7.2 What We Cannot Yet Answer
We have good generative models, but three big inferential questions remain:

Given a real network, which model generated it? Model selection requires likelihood comparison (BIC/AIC) or Bayesian approaches.
How do we find communities in an observed network? SBM gives us the generative story, but inference (finding \(z\) from \(G\)) is hard computationally. This is the community detection problem; the focus of the next lecture.
How do we predict missing edges or node labels? Even with a good model, predicting new edges (link prediction) or unseen node attributes (semi-supervised classification) requires additional ML machinery.

These three questions; community detection, link prediction, node classification; are the subject of Lecture 3: Machine Learning with Network Data.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
Before diving into definitions, it is worth asking: why is the study of networks a distinct field at all? Most economic models treat agents as interacting through aggregate prices; a firm responds to market wages, not to the specific workers it employs. Most statistical models treat observations as i.i.d. draws. Neither framework is adequate when the identity of who you are connected to fundamentally changes outcomes.
Consider a few canonical examples:









Setting
Nodes
Edges
Key question




Labour market
Workers, firms
Employment relations
Why do referred workers earn more?


Epidemiology
People
Physical contact
Who to vaccinate first?


Finance
Banks
Interbank loans
Which bank’s failure is systemic?


Supply chain
Firms
Input–output flows
Where does a shock propagate?


Social media
Users
Follows/retweets
How does misinformation spread?



What all of these have in common: the structure of connections; not just their average density; determines outcomes. A disease spreading through a hub-and-spoke airline network behaves very differently from one spreading through a lattice. A worker connected to a structurally diverse set of acquaintances finds jobs faster than one embedded in a tightly clustered clique, even holding the number of connections fixed.






NoteCourse perspective



This is not primarily a machine learning course. We study networks from an economics angle: we want to model strategic behaviour, predict equilibria, and use the structure of networks to understand data on economic and social activity. Machine learning tools (GNNs, embeddings) appear later as instruments for prediction, not as the main object of study.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
2.1 From Bridges to Graphs
The history of graph theory begins with a practical puzzle. In 1736, the city of Königsberg (now Kaliningrad) was divided by the Pregel River into four landmasses connected by seven bridges. Citizens wondered: is there a walk that crosses each bridge exactly once?
Leonhard Euler proved the answer is no, and in doing so laid the foundation for graph theory. His key insight: the problem is purely topological. The specific geography is irrelevant; what matters is only which landmasses are connected, and how many times. He observed that such a walk (now called an Eulerian path) requires every node except the start and end to have an even number of edges. Königsberg has four nodes, all with odd degree; so no such walk exists.
The abstraction Euler introduced; a set of nodes connected by edges; is exactly the graph. Every network model in this course is a specialisation of this same idea.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
2.2 Formal Definitions
A graph \(G = (V, E)\) consists of:

\(V\): a set of vertices (nodes), \(|V| = n\)
\(E\): a set of edges (links), \(|E| = m\)

For an undirected, unweighted graph, edges are unordered pairs \(\{i, j\} \in E\), and the adjacency matrix is:
\[A_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \{i,j\} \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]
Three key properties of this matrix:

Binary: \(A_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}\)
Symmetric: \(A_{ij} = A_{ji}\) (since edges have no direction)
Zero diagonal: \(A_{ii} = 0\) (no self-loops)


2.2.1 Variants Worth Knowing
Directed graphs: edges are ordered pairs \((i, j) \in E\), so \(A\) is no longer symmetric. Useful for citations, Twitter follows, river flows.
Weighted graphs: \(A_{ij} = w_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}\) records edge strength (trade volume, message frequency, road capacity).
Bipartite graphs: \(V\) is partitioned into two disjoint sets \(V_1, V_2\) with edges only between sets (actors ↔︎ films, buyers ↔︎ sellers, genes ↔︎ diseases).
Multigraphs: multiple edges between the same pair of nodes are allowed. We will mostly stay away from these, but they appear naturally in citation data.


Code
par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(1, 1, 3, 1))

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
3.1 Definition and Basic Identities
Describing a network by its full adjacency matrix \(A\) is informative but unwieldy. The first summary statistic is the degree of a node: the number of edges it has.
\[k_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\]
For weighted networks this generalises to strength: \(s_i = \sum_j A_{ij} w_{ij}\).
Two fundamental identities follow immediately from the definition:
Handshaking lemma: every edge contributes one to each endpoint’s degree, so the sum of degrees counts each edge twice:
\[\sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i = 2m \implies \langle k \rangle = \frac{2m}{n}\]
Density: the fraction of possible edges that actually exist:
\[\rho = \frac{m}{\binom{n}{2}} = \frac{2m}{n(n-1)} = \frac{\langle k \rangle}{n-1}\]
Real networks are almost always sparse: \(\rho \to 0\) as \(n \to \infty\), meaning \(m = O(n)\) rather than \(m = O(n^2)\). This has enormous computational implications — it is what makes sparse matrix formats and igraph’s C-level operations practical.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
3.2 Directed Degree
In a directed graph, a node has three degree notions:
\[k_i^{\text{out}} = \sum_j A_{ij}, \quad k_i^{\text{in}} = \sum_j A_{ji}, \quad k_i^{\text{tot}} = |\{j : A_{ij} = 1 \text{ or } A_{ji} = 1\}|\]
The conservation law: \(\sum_i k_i^{\text{out}} = \sum_i k_i^{\text{in}} = m\), so average in-degree always equals average out-degree.
In citation networks (PS1 Q4): \(k_i^{\text{out}}\) = number of papers cited by \(i\) (bibliography size); \(k_i^{\text{in}}\) = number of papers that cite \(i\) (citations received, often called “in-degree” or “citation count”).

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
3.3 The Degree Distribution
A single number like \(\langle k \rangle\) conceals crucial heterogeneity. The degree distribution \(p(k) = \Pr(K = k)\) gives the full picture.
Why does this matter? Consider two networks, both with \(n = 1490\) nodes and \(\langle k \rangle = 25.6\) (the 2004 political blogs network studied by Adamic & Glance). The maximum degree in that network was \(k_{\max} = 351\); more than 13 times the mean. Linear-scale histograms make this invisible. The right tool is a log–log plot of the complementary CDF:
\[\bar{F}(k) = \Pr(K \geq k)\]
If this is linear on a log–log scale, the degree distribution follows a power law: \(p(k) \propto k^{-\gamma}\). We will study why this emerges and what it implies in Lecture 2 (Section 4: Preferential Attachment).


Code
## Load American75 .mat file
mat  <- readMat("data/facebook_small/American75.mat")
A_sp <- mat$A                        # sparse matrix (dgCMatrix or similar)

# Build igraph from sparse adjacency
A_trip <- as(A_sp, "TsparseMatrix")  # converts to triplet form (i, j, x)
edges_fb <- cbind(A_trip@i + 1L, A_trip@j + 1L)   # 1-indexed
g <- graph_from_edgelist(edges_fb, directed = FALSE)
g <- simplify(g)                     # remove duplicates / self-loops

degs   <- degree(g)
n_g    <- vcount(g)
mean_k <- mean(degs)

## CCDF
ccdf_k   <- sort(unique(degs))
ccdf_val <- sapply(ccdf_k, function(k) mean(degs >= k))

## Plots
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

# Left: linear histogram
hist(
  degs, breaks = 40,
  col    = "#4C72B0", border = "white",
  xlab   = "Degree k",
  ylab   = "Count",
  main   = sprintf("Degree histogram\n(n=%d, <k>=%.1f)", n_g, mean_k)
)

# Right: log-log CCDF
plot(
  ccdf_k, ccdf_val,
  log  = "xy",
  pch  = 19, cex = 0.5, col = adjustcolor("#C44E52", alpha.f = 0.7),
  xlab = "Degree k",
  ylab = expression(bar(F)(k) == Pr(K >= k)),
  main = "Complementary CDF (log-log)"
)
grid()

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
4.1 Walks, Paths, and Geodesics
Moving from who is connected (degree) to how things flow requires thinking about paths through the network.
Formally, for an undirected graph:

A walk is any sequence of adjacent nodes \(i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_k\) where \(\{i_t, i_{t+1}\} \in E\) for all \(t\). Nodes and edges may repeat.
A path is a walk where all nodes are distinct.
A cycle is a closed path: \(i_1 = i_k\) with all intermediate nodes distinct.
The geodesic distance \(\ell_{ij}\) is the length of the shortest path between \(i\) and \(j\). If no path exists, \(\ell_{ij} = \infty\).

The diameter of a graph is the longest geodesic across all pairs: \[\text{diam}(G) = \max_{i \neq j} \ell_{ij}\] where we exclude \(\ell_{ij} = \infty\) (i.e., we measure within the largest connected component).
The mean geodesic distance (average path length) is: \[\langle \ell \rangle = \frac{1}{n(n-1)} \sum_{i \neq j} \ell_{ij}\]

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
section: 4.2 Why Distance Matters: The Small World Phenomenon
text:
4.2 Why Distance Matters: The Small World Phenomenon
In 1967, sociologist Stanley Milgram asked 296 residents of Wichita and Omaha to route a letter to a specific target in Boston; but only by passing it to someone they knew personally. Of the 64 letters that arrived, the median chain length was 5.5 intermediaries; giving rise to the phrase “six degrees of separation.”
(The study has since been critiqued: the 78% non-completion rate is not random, and the participants who succeeded were likely unusually well-connected. But the qualitative finding holds in larger digital datasets.)
A back-of-envelope argument explains why short paths are possible. Suppose each person has \(\gamma\) friends, with no overlap between social circles (a strong assumption). Then:

At distance 1: you can reach \(\gamma\) people
At distance 2: \(\gamma^2\) people
At distance \(d\): \(\gamma^d\) people

Setting \(\gamma^d = n\) and solving: \(d = \frac{\ln n}{\ln \gamma}\). For the US population \(n \approx 3 \times 10^8\):
\[d = \frac{\ln(3 \times 10^8)}{\ln \gamma}\]
Milgram’s result of \(d \approx 6\) implies \(\gamma \approx \exp\!\left(\frac{\ln(3\times 10^8)}{6}\right) \approx 53\) friends. That seems large; but notice Facebook mutual friends don’t count (weak ties, not acquaintances). The calculation is sensitive to the definition of “friend.”






ImportantThe key takeaway



Networks are “small” when the diameter \(D = O(\log n)\) rather than \(O(n)\). This exponential expansion along paths is what makes diseases, information, and prices propagate so quickly through real-world networks.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
section: 4.3 Computing Distances: BFS and the Facebook Networks
text:
4.3 Computing Distances: BFS and the Facebook Networks
Breadth-first search (BFS) from a single source computes all distances in \(O(n + m)\) time. For large graphs, computing all \(\binom{n}{2}\) pairwise distances by running BFS from every node costs \(O(n(n+m))\); feasible for \(n \lesssim 10^4\), but not for the full Facebook100 dataset.
PS1, Question 1 asks you to compute diameter and mean geodesic distance for each of the 84 Facebook university networks. The key approximation: sample \(s\) random source nodes and average; the error is \(O(1/\sqrt{s})\).


Code
## ---- Helper: sample average path length ----
sample_apl <- function(g, n_samples = 200, seed = 42) {
  set.seed(seed)
  comps <- components(g)
  lcc   <- induced_subgraph(g, which(comps$membership == which.max(comps$csize)))

  n_lcc   <- vcount(lcc)
  sources <- sample(seq_len(n_lcc), min(n_samples, n_lcc))

  total <- 0; count <- 0
  for (src in sources) {
    dists <- distances(lcc, v = src, mode = "out")
    finite_d <- dists[dists > 0 & is.finite(dists)]
    total <- total + sum(finite_d)
    count <- count + length(finite_d)
  }
  if (count == 0) return(NA_real_)
  total / count
}

## ---- Helper: double-sweep diameter lower bound ----
approx_diameter <- function(g) {
  comps <- components(g)
  lcc   <- induced_subgraph(g, which(comps$membership == which.max(comps$csize)))

  # Step 1: BFS from node 1
  d1 <- distances(lcc, v = 1)
  v  <- which.max(d1)

  # Step 2: BFS from v
  d2 <- distances(lcc, v = v)
  max(d2[is.finite(d2)])
}

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
5.1 Degree Centrality Is Not Enough
Degree captures local connectivity; how many direct neighbours a node has. But in many settings, influence is determined by position in the network structure, not just by raw connectivity.
Consider a node with degree 5 sitting at the junction between two otherwise disconnected communities, versus a node with degree 10 embedded in a tightly knit clique. The former is far more influential for information flow, despite lower degree.
This motivates centrality measures that capture global structural importance.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
5.2 Betweenness Centrality
The betweenness centrality of node \(k\) counts how often \(k\) lies on the shortest path between other pairs of nodes:
\[B_k = \frac{1}{\binom{n-1}{2}} \sum_{\substack{i \neq j \\ k \notin \{i,j\}}} \frac{P_k(i,j)}{P(i,j)}\]
where \(P(i,j)\) is the number of geodesic paths between \(i\) and \(j\), and \(P_k(i,j)\) is the number that pass through \(k\). The normalisation by \(\binom{n-1}{2}\) makes \(B_k \in [0, 1]\).
Interpretation: a node with high betweenness is a broker; it controls traffic between otherwise separate parts of the network. Removing such nodes tends to dramatically fragment the graph.

5.2.1 The Medici Example
15th-century Florence witnessed the rise of the Medici family from relative obscurity to political and economic dominance. The puzzle: many other families were wealthier and had longer aristocratic pedigrees.
Padgett & Ansell (1993) digitised the marriage network of Florentine families and computed betweenness centralities. The Medici had \(B_{\text{Medici}} = 0.522\) — the highest of any family, by a wide margin. The Medici were positioned at the structural centre of the marriage network, serving as the broker connecting families that could not directly influence each other. Their political rise was not incidental: it was written into the topology of the network.






TipBetweenness as an economic asset



High betweenness creates information brokerage: brokers hear news, prices, and opportunities before they diffuse to the rest of the network. It also creates structural holes (Burt 1992): the broker can play parties off against each other when they cannot coordinate directly.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
5.3 Closeness Centrality
Closeness centrality measures how quickly a node can reach all others:
\[C_k = \frac{n-1}{\sum_{j \neq k} \ell_{kj}}\]
A node with high closeness sits near the “centre of gravity” of the network and can spread information to all others quickly. Unlike betweenness, it does not depend on being a bottleneck; it rewards being well-connected on average.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
5.4 Eigenvector Centrality and PageRank
Both betweenness and closeness treat all paths equally. But a link from a high-status node should count more than a link from a peripheral one. This motivates eigenvector centrality:
\[x_i = \frac{1}{\lambda} \sum_j A_{ij} x_j \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad Ax = \lambda x\]
The centrality vector \(x\) is the leading eigenvector of \(A\) (by the Perron–Frobenius theorem, this has all non-negative entries for connected graphs). Each node’s score is proportional to the sum of its neighbours’ scores.
PageRank (Brin & Page, 1998) adds a damping factor to handle dangling nodes and make the walk ergodic:
\[r_i = \frac{1-d}{n} + d \sum_j A_{ji} \frac{r_j}{k_j^{\text{out}}}\]
With damping \(d = 0.85\): with probability \(d\) a random walker follows an outgoing link; with probability \(1-d\) it teleports to a random node. PageRank is the stationary distribution of this random walk.


Code

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
6.1 Motivation: Triangles Are Everywhere
Degree and betweenness describe individual nodes. A different question asks about the local structure around a node: to what extent are my neighbours also connected to each other?
This matters because:

Trust propagation: if A trusts B and A trusts C, there is social pressure for B and C to also form a relationship; what Simmel called the principle of triadic closure.
Community detection: clusters in networks are regions of high local clustering separated by sparse cross-community edges.
Model validation: the Erdős–Rényi model predicts \(C \to 0\) as \(n \to \infty\), but real networks maintain high clustering. This falsifies ER as a realistic model.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
6.2 Global Clustering (Transitivity)
The global clustering coefficient (or transitivity) is the fraction of connected triples that are closed into triangles:
\[C(A) = \frac{\text{number of triangles} \times 3}{\text{number of connected triples}} = \frac{\sum_{ijk} A_{ij} A_{jk} A_{ki}}{\sum_{ijk} A_{ij} A_{jk}}\]
The factor of 3 accounts for the three different “pivot” nodes in each triangle (each triangle contributes to three distinct connected triples). This makes \(C \in [0, 1]\).
In matrix terms, the numerator is \(\text{tr}(A^3)\) (each walk \(i \to j \to k \to i\) of length 3 is a triangle), and the denominator is \(\sum_{i} k_i(k_i - 1)\).

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
6.3 Local Clustering Coefficient
The local clustering coefficient for node \(i\) with degree \(k_i \geq 2\) is:
\[C_i(A) = \frac{\text{edges among neighbours of } i}{\binom{k_i}{2}} = \frac{2 L_i}{k_i(k_i - 1)}\]
where \(L_i\) is the number of edges among \(i\)’s \(k_i\) neighbours.
The average clustering coefficient \(\bar{C} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_i C_i\) weights all nodes equally regardless of degree, giving more weight to low-degree nodes compared to the global \(C(A)\).






Note\(C\) vs \(\bar{C}\): which to use?



\(C(A)\) (global transitivity) is the right metric when you care about the fraction of triangles in the graph as a whole. \(\bar{C}\) is more sensitive to the local structure around typical low-degree nodes. They can differ substantially: hubs with high degree contribute little to \(\bar{C}\) (since their many neighbours are unlikely to all be connected) but dominate \(C(A)\).




Code
clustering_report <- function(g, name) {
  c_global <- transitivity(g, type = "global")
  c_avg    <- transitivity(g, type = "average")   # avg local CC, isolates excluded
  deg      <- degree(g)
  cc_local <- transitivity(g, type = "local")

  valid <- which(deg >= 2 & !is.nan(cc_local))
  corr  <- cor(deg[valid], cc_local[valid])

  cat(sprintf("%s:\n", name))
  cat(sprintf("  Global transitivity C(A) = %.4f\n", c_global))
  cat(sprintf("  Avg local CC  C_bar      = %.4f\n", c_avg))
  cat(sprintf("  Corr(degree, local CC)   = %.4f%s\n",
              corr,
              ifelse(corr < 0, "  (hubs less clustered)", "")))
}

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
7.1 Granovetter’s Paradox
One of the most celebrated findings in sociology (Granovetter 1973, the most cited paper in the sociology of networks) concerns an apparent paradox in job referrals:

Workers in the labour market find jobs primarily through referrals from acquaintances; not from close friends.

Why would weaker connections be more useful? The answer lies in the topology of clustering.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
7.2 Triadic Closure and Local Bridges
Define a strong tie as a close friendship and a weak tie as an acquaintance. The Strong Triadic Closure (STC) property says: if node \(A\) has strong ties to both \(B\) and \(C\), then there should be at least a weak tie between \(B\) and \(C\).
Intuitively: if \(A\) is close friends with both \(B\) and \(C\), it is socially costly and informationally inconsistent for \(B\) and \(C\) to not even know each other.
A local bridge between \(A\) and \(B\) is an edge whose removal would increase \(\ell_{AB}\) substantially (say, to \(> 2\)). Local bridges connect otherwise distant parts of the network.
Claim: if STC holds and \(A\) has at least two strong ties, then any local bridge involving \(A\) must be a weak tie.
Proof sketch: suppose \(A\)–\(B\) is a strong tie and a local bridge. By STC, for every other strong neighbour \(C\) of \(A\), there must be an edge \(B\)–\(C\). But then removing \(A\)–\(B\) would not increase \(\ell_{BC}\) much (path goes through \(A\)–\(C\)–… to \(B\)), contradicting “local bridge.” Contradiction. \(\square\)
Implication for job referrals: close friends move in the same social circles. They know the same people, the same managers, the same opportunities. An acquaintance (weak tie) is embedded in a different cluster and therefore carries non-redundant information about job opportunities you haven’t heard of.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
7.3 LinkedIn RCT Validation
Rajkumar et al. (2022) ran a massive randomised controlled trial: over 2015–2019, LinkedIn adjusted its “People You May Know” algorithm for 20+ million users. Some users received more weak-tie suggestions, others more strong-tie suggestions.
Key results: - New connections formed from weak-tie suggestions led to more job transmissions than those from strong-tie suggestions - But the relationship is an inverted U: moderately weak ties (around 10 mutual connections) were optimal - Ties with 0 mutual connections (pure strangers) were not useful either - The effect was strongest in high-technology sectors
This is a rare case where a classic sociological theory was validated at scale using a pre-registered experiment with billions of data points.


Code
# Two clusters + one bridge

  # Local bridge: node 3 (A) -- node 7 (B)

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
text:
8.1 Braess’s Paradox
Consider a road network where one unit of traffic must travel from \(s\) to \(t\). Two routes exist:

Route 1: \(s \xrightarrow{x_1} A \xrightarrow{1} t\), cost \(= x_1 + 1\)
Route 2: \(s \xrightarrow{1} B \xrightarrow{x_2} t\), cost \(= 1 + x_2\)

where \(x_1, x_2\) are flows on the congested links (cost = flow on those links).
At the social optimum: split traffic equally. \(x_1 = x_2 = 1/2\). Total cost \(= \frac{3}{2}\).
At the Nash equilibrium: each infinitesimal driver chooses the route with lowest cost given others’ choices. Equilibrium also has \(x_1 = x_2 = 1/2\) and total cost \(= \frac{3}{2}\).
Now add a zero-cost road from \(A\) to \(B\). Three routes now exist, including \(s \to A \to B \to t\) with cost \(x_1 + 0 + x_2\). At the new Nash equilibrium: everyone takes the \(s \to A \to B \to t\) route, giving \(x_1 = x_2 = 1\) and cost \(= 2 > \frac{3}{2}\).
Adding a road made everyone worse off. This is Braess’s paradox (1968), and it has been observed empirically in Seoul, Stuttgart, and New York.
The Price of Anarchy quantifies the efficiency loss:
\[\text{PoA} = \frac{C(\text{Nash})}{C(\text{Social Optimum})}\]
For the Braess example: \(\text{PoA} = 2 / (3/2) = 4/3\).






NoteWhy this matters



Braess’s paradox shows that network structure interacts with strategic behaviour in non-obvious ways. Infrastructure investment, drug trafficking crackdowns (Dell 2015), and platform design all face versions of this problem. Understanding when adding capacity helps versus hurts requires understanding the network topology.

title: 01: Introduction to Networks
section: 9.2 The Critical Gap: Where Do These Numbers Come From?
text:
9.2 The Critical Gap: Where Do These Numbers Come From?
We can compute all of these measures on any real network. But then what?
Suppose we find that the American75 Facebook network has \(C = 0.15\). Is that high? Low? Expected? To answer this, we need a null model; a probability distribution over graphs with the same basic parameters (\(n\), \(\langle k \rangle\)) against which to compare the observed statistic.
This is the core motivation for random graph models, the subject of Lecture 2.
Key questions that random graphs will let us answer:

What clustering coefficient should we expect from a network with \(n\) nodes and mean degree \(\langle k \rangle\)? (Erdős–Rényi model → answer: \(C \approx 0\))
Why does clustering in real networks vastly exceed the ER prediction? (Watts–Strogatz small-world model)
Why do real networks have such heavy-tailed degree distributions? (Barabási–Albert preferential attachment)
Can we infer latent community structure from the observed edge pattern? (Stochastic Block Model)



Code

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
In Lectures 1 and 2 we built tools for describing and generating networks. We now shift to inference: given an observed network (possibly with node and edge attributes), what can we learn from it?
Three classes of inference problem:








Task
Input
Output




Community detection
Graph \(G\)
Partition of \(V\) into groups


Node classification
Graph \(G\), partial labels \(y_i\)
Labels for unlabelled nodes


Link prediction
Graph \(G\) (incomplete)
Probability of unobserved edges



And one representation problem underlying all of them:
Node embedding | Graph \(G\) (+ features) | Vectors \(z_i \in \mathbb{R}^d\) |
These are not independent problems. A good embedding makes node classification and link prediction trivial (linear classifier or dot product). Community detection can be cast as clustering in embedding space.



Real network data is almost always incomplete in multiple ways:

Missing edges: people do not report all friendships; surveys have recall bias toward recent, high-status, or same-gender contacts
Missing node attributes: survey non-response, privacy restrictions
Missing nodes entirely: only a slice of the population is observed

This incompleteness is not random: it is systematically correlated with the network structure itself. Methods that assume missing-at-random can be badly misleading.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
2.1 The Problem: Finding Latent Groups
A community is a set of nodes that are more densely connected internally than to the rest of the graph. This is an intuitive but deliberately vague definition.
Why do we care?

Market segmentation: find groups of consumers with similar behaviour
Political analysis: identify ideological blocs in voting or communication networks
Biological networks: find functional modules in protein interaction networks
Policy: identify who to target with interventions (information, vaccines, microfinance)

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
2.2 Modularity: A Principled Score
The standard measure of community quality is modularity \(Q\) (Newman & Girvan 2004). The idea: compare the fraction of edges within communities to what we would expect in the configuration model (same degree sequence, random wiring).
\[Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j} \left(A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}\right) \mathbb{1}[c(i) = c(j)]\]
Equivalently, summing over communities \(c\):
\[Q = \sum_c \left[\frac{m_c}{m} - \left(\frac{\kappa_c}{2m}\right)^2\right]\]
where \(m_c\) = edges within community \(c\), and \(\kappa_c = \sum_{i \in c} k_i\) = total degree of community \(c\).
Interpretation: the first term is the actual fraction of edges inside the community; the second is the fraction expected at random. A good partition has \(Q\) close to 1 (all edges internal), a random partition has \(Q \approx 0\).






NoteWhy the configuration model as null?



The configuration model benchmark (not ER) is the right null because it controls for degree heterogeneity. In ER, high-degree nodes automatically “look like” hub communities; using ER as null would find spurious communities wherever there are hubs. Conditioning on degree removes this confound.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
2.3 The Louvain Algorithm
Finding the partition maximising \(Q\) is NP-hard. The Louvain method (Blondel et al. 2008) is the standard greedy approximation:
Phase 1 (local optimisation): Start with each node in its own community. For each node \(i\), compute the \(\Delta Q\) gain from moving \(i\) into each neighbouring community:
\[\Delta Q = \frac{k_{i,\text{in}}}{m} - \frac{k_i \Sigma_{\text{tot}}}{2m^2}\]
where \(k_{i,\text{in}}\) = edges from \(i\) to the target community, \(\Sigma_{\text{tot}}\) = total degree of the target community. Move \(i\) to the community with largest positive \(\Delta Q\). Repeat until no improvement exists.
Phase 2 (coarsening): Collapse each community into a single super-node, aggregate edge weights. Repeat Phase 1 on this coarser graph.
Complexity: \(O(n \log n)\) in practice. Works on networks with millions of nodes.






WarningResolution limit



Modularity has a fundamental flaw: it cannot detect communities smaller than \(\sqrt{m}\) edges, because merging two small communities can increase \(Q\) even if they have no internal connections. For networks with \(m = 10^6\), communities smaller than \(\sim 1000\) edges are invisible to any modularity-based method.
The Leiden algorithm (Traag, Waltman & van Eck 2019) fixes a related bug in Louvain (communities can become internally disconnected) and uses a refined local search. For serious community detection work, prefer Leiden.




Code
set.seed(42)

k_comm <- 4L
n_per  <- 60L
n_tot  <- k_comm * n_per
p_in   <- 0.20
p_out  <- 0.02

pref_mat    <- matrix(p_out, nrow = k_comm, ncol = k_comm)
diag(pref_mat) <- p_in

# Leiden (requires leidenAlg or igraph >= 1.3 which wraps it)

leiden_mem <- tryCatch(
  membership(cluster_leiden(g_sbm)) - 1L,
  error = function(e) {

    louvain_mem

)

cat(sprintf("Community detection on n=%d, k=%d SBM (p_in=%.2f, p_out=%.2f):\n",

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
2.4 SBM Inference as an Alternative
Modularity optimisation lacks a generative model; we are maximising a score, not fitting a probability model. The SBM inference approach (from Lecture 2) is more principled:

Specify a generative model (\(k\) communities, mixing matrix \(M\))
Write down the likelihood \(\mathcal{L}(G \mid z, M)\)
Maximise over both \(z\) and \(M\); MLE gives \(\hat{M}_{uv} = E_{uv}/N_{uv}\)
Use BIC to choose \(k\): \(\text{BIC} = -2 \ln \hat{\mathcal{L}} + p \ln n\) where \(p = k + \binom{k}{2}\) parameters

Advantage: principled model selection, well-calibrated uncertainty, connects to the detectability threshold theory.
Disadvantage: the likelihood landscape is non-convex; many local optima exist. Run multiple random initialisations and select the best.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
3.1 The Semi-Supervised Setup
Given a graph \(G = (V, E)\) where a small subset \(V_L \subset V\) has known labels \(y_i \in \{1, \ldots, K\}\), predict labels for \(V_U = V \setminus V_L\).
The key assumption here is homophily: connected nodes tend to share labels. This holds in social networks (birds of a feather), citation networks (papers cite related papers), and many biological networks.






WarningWhen homophily fails



Fraud detection networks often exhibit heterophily: fraudsters connect to legitimate users in order to appear trustworthy. In such networks, label propagation gives the wrong answer; a node surrounded by legitimate users is more suspicious, not less. Always validate the homophily assumption.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
3.2 Label Propagation (Zhu & Ghahramani 2002)
The simplest and most robust algorithm:
Initialisation: \(F_i = y_i\) for labelled nodes (one-hot), uniform for unlabelled.
Update rule (iterative): \[F_i \leftarrow \frac{1}{k_i} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} F_j, \quad \forall i \in V_U\]
Clamping: after each step, reset labelled nodes to their true values.
Convergence: iterate until \(\|F^{(t+1)} - F^{(t)}\|_\infty < \epsilon\).
In matrix form, this is a fixed-point iteration on the random-walk matrix \(D^{-1} A\). The closed-form solution is:
\[F_U = (I - D_{UU}^{-1} A_{UU})^{-1} D_{UU}^{-1} A_{UL} Y_L\]
Why it works: the random-walk matrix \(D^{-1}A\) is the transition matrix of a lazy random walk. Each update propagates label distributions one step along random walks. Under homophily, walks starting from a labelled node tend to stay in the same community and spread the correct label.
Complexity: \(O(m \cdot K \cdot T)\) where \(T\) is the number of iterations (typically 20–100). Very fast for sparse graphs.


Code
label_propagation <- function(g, Y, labeled_mask, max_iter = 200, tol = 1e-8) {
  # Y           : (n x K) numeric matrix, one-hot for labelled nodes
  # labeled_mask: logical vector length n
  n    <- vcount(g)
  K    <- ncol(Y)
  F    <- Y
  nbrs <- lapply(seq_len(n), function(v) neighbors(g, v))
  degs <- pmax(sapply(nbrs, length), 1L)

  for (iter in seq_len(max_iter)) {
    F_old <- F
    for (v in seq_len(n)) {
      if (labeled_mask[v] || length(nbrs[[v]]) == 0) next
      F[v, ] <- colSums(F[nbrs[[v]], , drop = FALSE]) / degs[v]
    }
    F[labeled_mask, ] <- Y[labeled_mask, ]
    if (max(abs(F - F_old)) < tol) break
  }
  F
}

set.seed(0)
n_per_c  <- 40L; k_c <- 3L; n_c <- n_per_c * k_c
pref_c   <- matrix(0.02, k_c, k_c); diag(pref_c) <- 0.3
g_c      <- sample_sbm(n_c, pref = pref_c, block.sizes = rep(n_per_c, k_c))
true_labels_c <- rep(seq_len(k_c) - 1L, each = n_per_c)   # 0-indexed

label_fracs  <- c(0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.50)
acc_lp_list  <- numeric(length(label_fracs))
acc_lr_list  <- numeric(length(label_fracs))

# Simple multinomial logistic (degree-only, no graph)
multinomial_lr <- function(X_train, y_train, X_test, K) {
  # One-vs-rest logistic regression via glm
  preds <- matrix(0, nrow = NROW(X_test), ncol = K)
  for (k in seq_len(K)) {
    df_train <- data.frame(y = as.integer(y_train == (k - 1L)), x = X_train)
    fit <- glm(y ~ x, data = df_train, family = binomial())
    preds[, k] <- predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(x = X_test), type = "response")
  }
  apply(preds, 1, which.max) - 1L
}

deg_feat <- degree(g_c)

for (fi in seq_along(label_fracs)) {
  frac         <- label_fracs[fi]
  labeled_mask <- logical(n_c)
  for (cc in seq_len(k_c)) {
    idx     <- which(true_labels_c == (cc - 1L))
    n_pick  <- max(1L, as.integer(length(idx) * frac))
    labeled_mask[sample(idx, n_pick)] <- TRUE
  }

  # Label propagation
  Y <- matrix(0, n_c, k_c)
  for (i in seq_len(n_c)) Y[i, true_labels_c[i] + 1L] <- 1.0
  F0 <- Y; F0[!labeled_mask, ] <- 1.0 / k_c
  F_pred <- label_propagation(g_c, F0, labeled_mask)
  pred_lp <- apply(F_pred, 1, which.max) - 1L
  acc_lp_list[fi] <- mean(pred_lp[!labeled_mask] == true_labels_c[!labeled_mask])

  # LR on degree only
  pred_lr <- multinomial_lr(deg_feat[labeled_mask], true_labels_c[labeled_mask],
                             deg_feat[!labeled_mask], k_c)
  acc_lr_list[fi] <- mean(pred_lr == true_labels_c[!labeled_mask])
}

par(mar = c(4, 4, 4, 1))
plot(label_fracs * 100, acc_lp_list,
     type = "b", pch = 19, cex = 0.8, col = "#C44E52", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "% nodes labelled", ylab = "Accuracy on unlabelled nodes",
     main = sprintf("Label Propagation vs. No-Graph Baseline\n(n=%d, k=%d communities)",
                    n_c, k_c),
     ylim = c(0, 1.05))
lines(label_fracs * 100, acc_lr_list, type = "b", pch = 15, cex = 0.8,
      col = "#4C72B0", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
abline(h = 1 / k_c, col = "gray", lty = 3, lwd = 1.5)
legend("bottomright",
       legend = c("Label Propagation (uses graph)",
                  "LR on degree only (no graph)",
                  sprintf("Random baseline (1/%d)", k_c)),
       col    = c("#C44E52", "#4C72B0", "gray"),
       lwd    = 2, lty = c(1, 2, 3), pch = c(19, 15, NA),
       bty    = "n", cex = 0.9)

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
section: 3.3 When to Use What: A Decision Ladder
text:
3.3 When to Use What: A Decision Ladder
Before reaching for a GNN, always try the simpler methods first:

Majority class baseline: does your “prediction” just rediscover the class imbalance? Always compute this.
LR on node features only: how much does the graph add, beyond features?
Label propagation (graph only): how much do features add, beyond graph?
ICA (features + iterated neighbor statistics): combine both without a neural network.
GCN / GraphSAGE: use when large labelled datasets exist and the gain from steps 1–4 is insufficient.

Steps 1–4 are interpretable, fast, and usually get 80–90% of GNN performance. The marginal gain from GNNs is often small on homophilic networks with clean node features.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
4.1 Problem Setup
Given the observed graph \(G = (V, E)\), predict which non-edges \((i, j) \notin E\) are “true” edges; either missing from our measurement, or likely to form in the future.
Evaluation protocol: 1. Hold out 10–20% of edges as positive test examples 2. Sample an equal number of negative examples (non-edges) 3. Score all candidate pairs with a similarity function \(s(i, j)\) 4. Compute AUC-ROC: \(\Pr[s(\text{true edge}) > s(\text{non-edge})]\)
Negative sampling matters: random non-edges are too easy (most pairs have \(s \approx 0\)). Hard negatives at distance 2 or 3 give more realistic evaluation.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
4.2 Structural Similarity Scores
All heuristics score \((i, j)\) based on their common neighbourhood \(\mathcal{N}(i) \cap \mathcal{N}(j)\).
Common Neighbours (CN): \[s_{\text{CN}}(i,j) = |\mathcal{N}(i) \cap \mathcal{N}(j)|\]
Jaccard coefficient (normalises for hub effect): \[s_J(i,j) = \frac{|\mathcal{N}(i) \cap \mathcal{N}(j)|}{|\mathcal{N}(i) \cup \mathcal{N}(j)|}\]
Adamic–Adar (Adamic & Adar 2003): down-weight common neighbours with high degree (high-degree nodes are “common friends” who are friends with everyone and therefore carry less information):
\[s_{\text{AA}}(i,j) = \sum_{w \in \mathcal{N}(i) \cap \mathcal{N}(j)} \frac{1}{\log k_w}\]
Resource Allocation: stricter version, using \(1/k_w\) instead of \(1/\log k_w\).
Preferential Attachment: no common-neighbour computation needed, just: \[s_{\text{PA}}(i,j) = k_i \cdot k_j\]
PA is useful in scale-free networks: hubs grow by attaching to other hubs. It has \(O(1)\) computation per pair and scales to very large graphs.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
4.3 Global Similarity: Katz Index
Common-neighbour methods only look one hop away. The Katz index aggregates over all path lengths, weighted by a decay factor \(\beta < 1\):
\[K(i,j) = \sum_{l=1}^{\infty} \beta^l [A^l]_{ij} = [(I - \beta A)^{-1} - I]_{ij}\]
\([A^l]_{ij}\) counts the number of walks of length \(l\) from \(i\) to \(j\). The \(\beta\) discount ensures convergence (requires \(\beta < 1/\lambda_{\max}(A)\)).
Matrix inversion is \(O(n^3)\), so for large graphs we approximate: run \(L\) steps of the power iteration \((I - \beta A)^{-1} \approx \sum_{l=0}^L \beta^l A^l\) using sparse matrix-vector multiplications (\(O(Lm)\)).

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
4.4 Measuring Performance: The AUC
The ROC curve plots the true positive rate (TPR) against the false positive rate (FPR) as the score threshold varies. AUC is the area under this curve:
\[\text{AUC} = \Pr[s(\text{true edge}) > s(\text{random non-edge})]\]
AUC = 0.5: no better than random. AUC = 1.0: perfect separation.


Code
# ---- Similarity functions ----
cn_score <- function(g, u, v) {
  length(intersect(neighbors(g, u), neighbors(g, v)))
}

jaccard_score <- function(g, u, v) {
  nu <- neighbors(g, u); nv <- neighbors(g, v)
  denom <- length(union(nu, nv))
  if (denom == 0) return(0.0)
  length(intersect(nu, nv)) / denom
}

adamic_adar <- function(g, u, v) {
  common <- intersect(neighbors(g, u), neighbors(g, v))
  if (length(common) == 0) return(0.0)
  d_w <- degree(g, common)
  sum(ifelse(d_w > 1, 1 / log(d_w), 0))
}

pref_attach <- function(g, u, v) {
  degree(g, u) * degree(g, v)
}

# ---- AUC helper ----
auc_roc <- function(y_true, y_score) {
  # Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney estimator: P(score_pos > score_neg)
  pos <- y_score[y_true == 1]
  neg <- y_score[y_true == 0]
  if (length(pos) == 0 || length(neg) == 0) return(NaN)
  mean(outer(pos, neg, ">")) + 0.5 * mean(outer(pos, neg, "=="))
}

evaluate_lp <- function(g, score_fn, n_test = 500L, seed = 42L) {
  set.seed(seed)
  edges    <- as_edgelist(g, names = FALSE)  # (m x 2) matrix, 1-indexed
  n        <- vcount(g)
  n_test   <- min(n_test, nrow(edges) %/% 5L)
  test_idx <- sample(nrow(edges), n_test)
  test_pos <- edges[test_idx, , drop = FALSE]

  # Train graph: remove test edges
  train_g <- delete_edges(g, get.edge.ids(g, t(test_pos)))

  # Build edge set for fast lookup
  edge_set <- apply(edges, 1, function(r) paste(sort(r), collapse = "_"))
  edge_set  <- as.environment(as.list(setNames(rep(TRUE, length(edge_set)), edge_set)))

  # Sample negatives
  negs  <- matrix(0L, 0L, 2L)
  tries <- 0L
  while (nrow(negs) < n_test && tries < n_test * 200L) {
    u <- sample.int(n, 1L); v <- sample.int(n, 1L)
    key <- paste(sort(c(u, v)), collapse = "_")
    if (u != v && !exists(key, envir = edge_set, inherits = FALSE))
      negs <- rbind(negs, c(u, v))
    tries <- tries + 1L
  }

  y_true  <- c(rep(1L, n_test), rep(0L, nrow(negs)))
  y_score <- c(
    apply(test_pos, 1, function(r) score_fn(train_g, r[1], r[2])),
    apply(negs,     1, function(r) score_fn(train_g, r[1], r[2]))
  )
  auc_roc(y_true, y_score)
}

methods <- list(
  list(name = "Common Neighbours", fn = cn_score),
  list(name = "Jaccard",           fn = jaccard_score),
  list(name = "Adamic-Adar",       fn = adamic_adar),
  list(name = "Pref. Attachment",  fn = pref_attach)
)

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
5.1 Why Embeddings?
Structural similarity scores like Adamic–Adar are hand-crafted and task-specific. A more flexible approach: learn a mapping \(\text{ENC}: V \to \mathbb{R}^d\) such that structurally similar nodes have similar embeddings.
Then: - Link prediction: \(s(i, j) = \sigma(z_i^\top z_j)\) - Node classification: logistic regression on \(z_i\) - Community detection: \(k\)-means on \(\{z_i\}\) - Visualisation: 2D PCA or UMAP of \(\{z_i\}\)
The key design choice is what “similar” means; different definitions lead to different algorithms.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
section: 5.2 Shallow Embeddings: The Matrix Factorization View
text:
5.2 Shallow Embeddings: The Matrix Factorization View
Spectral embedding (Laplacian Eigenmaps): find vectors that minimise
\[\min_{Z} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \|z_i - z_j\|^2 \quad \text{s.t. } Z^\top D Z = I\]
The solution is the \(d\) smallest non-trivial eigenvectors of the normalised Laplacian \(L_{\text{sym}} = D^{-1/2}(D-A)D^{-1/2}\).
This is equivalent to truncated SVD on \(A_{\text{sym}} = D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}\). The top-\(d\) singular vectors of the normalised adjacency give embeddings where dot products approximate random-walk co-occurrence probabilities.
Limitation: these are transductive; the embedding is fixed for the training graph. A new node that joins the network needs to be re-embedded.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
5.3 DeepWalk: Random Walks as Sentences
Perozzi et al. (2014) drew an analogy between graphs and text: nodes are words, random walks are sentences, co-occurrence in a walk window is analogous to co-occurrence in a text context window.
Algorithm:

For each node \(v\), generate \(r\) random walks of length \(\ell\) starting at \(v\)
Treat each walk as a “sentence” of node IDs
Run skip-gram with negative sampling: maximise \[\sum_{v \in V} \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}_R(v)} \log \sigma(z_v^\top z_u)
+ \sum_{k=1}^K \mathbb{E}_{u_k \sim P_n} [\log \sigma(-z_v^\top z_{u_k})]\]

where \(\mathcal{N}_R(v)\) is the multi-set of nodes in the walk neighbourhood of \(v\) (within window \(w\)), and \(P_n\) is the noise distribution for negative sampling.
Why it works: nodes that frequently co-occur on short random walks; meaning they are in the same community or tightly connected subgraph; get assigned similar embedding vectors. The skip-gram objective factorises the PMI (pointwise mutual information) matrix of co-occurrences.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
5.4 Node2Vec: Interpolating BFS and DFS
DeepWalk uses uniform random walks. Node2Vec (Grover & Leskovec 2016) introduces a 2nd-order Markov bias controlled by two parameters:

Return parameter \(p\): probability of returning to the previous node (inversely proportional to \(1/p\)). High \(p\) → less backtracking.
In-out parameter \(q\): controls BFS vs DFS. \(q < 1\) → DFS (explore outward), \(q > 1\) → BFS (stay local).

At node \(v\), having arrived from node \(u\), transition to \(x\) with unnormalised probability:
\[\alpha_{pq}(u, x) = \begin{cases} 1/p & d(u, x) = 0 \;\text{(return)} \\ 1 & d(u, x) = 1 \;\text{(lateral)} \\ 1/q & d(u, x) = 2 \;\text{(outward)} \end{cases}\]
Why this matters:

BFS walks (\(q \gg 1\)): explore the local neighbourhood. Nodes with similar local structure (same community) get similar embeddings. Captures homophily.
DFS walks (\(q \ll 1\)): explore outward, sampling diverse parts of the graph. Nodes at the same role in different communities (e.g., all hub nodes) get similar embeddings. Captures structural equivalence.



Code
set.seed(0)
k_emb  <- 4L; n_e <- 30L; n_emb <- k_emb * n_e
pref_e <- matrix(0.02, k_emb, k_emb); diag(pref_e) <- 0.3
g_emb  <- sample_sbm(n_emb, pref = pref_e, block.sizes = rep(n_e, k_emb))
true_labels_e <- rep(seq_len(k_emb) - 1L, each = n_e)

# Build normalised adjacency A_norm = D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}
A_e     <- as_adjacency_matrix(g_emb, sparse = TRUE)
deg_e   <- Matrix::rowSums(A_e)
d_isqrt <- ifelse(deg_e > 0, 1 / sqrt(deg_e), 0)
D_isqrt <- Diagonal(x = d_isqrt)
A_norm  <- D_isqrt %*% A_e %*% D_isqrt

# Top-5 singular vectors via RSpectra (or base svd on small graph)
# For small n use base svd; for large use RSpectra::svds
svd_res <- if (requireNamespace("RSpectra", quietly = TRUE)) {
  RSpectra::svds(A_norm, k = 5L)
} else {
  sv <- svd(as.matrix(A_norm), nu = 5L, nv = 5L)
  list(u = sv$u, d = sv$d, v = sv$v)
}
# Skip leading (trivial) eigenvector; take cols 2 and 3
emb <- svd_res$u[, 2:3, drop = FALSE]

# Random walk helper
random_walk <- function(g, start, len) {
  walk <- integer(len)
  walk[1] <- start
  for (i in seq_len(len - 1)) {
    nb <- neighbors(g, walk[i])
    if (length(nb) == 0) { walk <- walk[seq_len(i)]; break }
    walk[i + 1] <- sample(nb, 1L)
  }
  walk
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 4, 1))

## (A) 2D spectral embedding
emb_cols <- c("#4C72B0", "#C44E52", "#55A868", "#DD8452")
plot(NULL, xlim = range(emb[, 1]) * 1.1, ylim = range(emb[, 2]) * 1.1,
     xlab = "Eigenvector 2", ylab = "Eigenvector 3",
     main = "2D Spectral Embedding of SBM\n(communities cluster by construction)")
for (cc in seq_len(k_emb)) {
  mask_c <- true_labels_e == (cc - 1L)
  points(emb[mask_c, 1], emb[mask_c, 2],
         pch = 19, cex = 0.9, col = adjustcolor(emb_cols[cc], 0.8))
}
legend("topright", legend = paste0("Community ", 0:(k_emb - 1)),
       col = emb_cols, pch = 19, bty = "n", cex = 0.85)

## (B) Random walk co-occurrence heatmap (4x4, aggregated by community)
set.seed(42)
n_walks  <- 30L; walk_len <- 15L; window <- 3L
walks    <- lapply(seq_len(n_emb), function(v)
  replicate(ceiling(n_walks / n_emb) + 1L,
            random_walk(g_emb, v, walk_len), simplify = FALSE))
walks    <- unlist(walks, recursive = FALSE)

cooc <- matrix(0, n_emb, n_emb)
for (w in walks) {
  lw <- length(w)
  for (i in seq_len(lw)) {
    js <- seq(max(1L, i - window), min(lw, i + window))
    js <- js[js != i]
    cooc[w[i], w[js]] <- cooc[w[i], w[js]] + 1L
  }
}

comm_cooc <- matrix(0, k_emb, k_emb)
for (u in seq_len(n_emb))
  for (v in seq_len(n_emb))
    comm_cooc[true_labels_e[u] + 1L, true_labels_e[v] + 1L] <-
      comm_cooc[true_labels_e[u] + 1L, true_labels_e[v] + 1L] + cooc[u, v]
comm_cooc <- comm_cooc / rowSums(comm_cooc)

image(t(comm_cooc[k_emb:1, ]), col = hcl.colors(20, "Blues", rev = TRUE),
      xaxt = "n", yaxt = "n",
      xlab = "Landing community", ylab = "Source community",
      main = "Random Walk Co-occurrence\n(diagonal = within-community)")
axis(1, at = seq(0, 1, length.out = k_emb), labels = paste0("C", 0:(k_emb-1)))
axis(2, at = seq(0, 1, length.out = k_emb), labels = paste0("C", (k_emb-1):0), las = 1)





Spectral embedding of an SBM. Communities cluster clearly in 2D.




Code
# Spectral embedding similarity: within vs cross community
within_sim <- c(); cross_sim <- c()
for (i in seq_len(n_emb)) {
  for (j in seq_len(n_emb)) {
    if (j <= i) next
    s <- sum(emb[i, ] * emb[j, ])
    if (true_labels_e[i] == true_labels_e[j]) within_sim <- c(within_sim, s)
    else                                       cross_sim  <- c(cross_sim,  s)
  }
}
cat(sprintf("Spectral embedding similarity:\n"))


Spectral embedding similarity:

cat(sprintf("  Within-community: mean = %.4f\n",  mean(within_sim)))


  Within-community: mean = 0.0158

cat(sprintf("  Cross-community:  mean = %.4f\n",  mean(cross_sim)))

            mean(within_sim) / abs(mean(cross_sim))))
