title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
5.5 Limitations of Shallow Embeddings
All methods so far produce transductive embeddings: one vector per node, learned on a specific graph. They cannot:

Embed new nodes without retraining
Incorporate rich node features (text, demographics, sensor readings)
Adapt to different downstream tasks (same embedding used for both link prediction and community detection)

This motivates inductive methods; specifically Graph Neural Networks.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
section: 6.1 The Core Idea: Learned Neighbourhood Aggregation
text:
6.1 The Core Idea: Learned Neighbourhood Aggregation
A GNN replaces the fixed aggregation of shallow methods with a learnable neighbourhood aggregation function:
\[h_v^{(l+1)} = \sigma\!\left(W^{(l)} \cdot \text{AGG}\!\left(\{h_u^{(l)} : u \in \mathcal{N}(v)\}\right) + B^{(l)} h_v^{(l)}\right)\]
where: - \(h_v^{(0)} = x_v\) (node features, or a one-hot vector if none exist) - \(W^{(l)}, B^{(l)}\) are learnable weight matrices, shared across all nodes - AGG is an aggregation function (mean, sum, max, attention-weighted) - \(\sigma\) is a non-linearity (ReLU, etc.)
The sharing of weights across nodes is what makes GNNs inductive: the same function is applied to every node’s neighbourhood, so it generalises to unseen nodes with the same feature distribution.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
6.2 Graph Convolutional Network (GCN, Kipf & Welling 2017)
The most widely used variant simplifies to:
\[H^{(l+1)} = \sigma\!\left(\hat{A} H^{(l)} W^{(l)}\right)\]
where \(\hat{A} = \tilde{D}^{-1/2} \tilde{A} \tilde{D}^{-1/2}\) is the symmetrically normalised adjacency with added self-loops (\(\tilde{A} = A + I\)).
Why self-loops? Without them, \(H^{(l+1)}_v\) depends only on neighbours, not on \(v\) itself. The self-loop ensures each node aggregates its own features together with its neighbours’.
Why symmetric normalisation? It prevents the embeddings of high-degree nodes from having artificially large magnitudes and makes gradient flow more stable. It also corresponds to the Laplacian smoothing filter.
Two-layer GCN for node classification (fully written out):
\[Z = \text{softmax}\!\left(\hat{A} \cdot \text{ReLU}\!\left(\hat{A} X W^{(0)}\right) W^{(1)}\right)\]
Train by minimising cross-entropy on labelled nodes: \(\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i \in V_L} \sum_k y_{ik} \ln Z_{ik}\)

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
6.3 GraphSAGE: Scalable Inductive Learning
GCN requires the full adjacency matrix \(\hat{A}\) during training. For a graph with \(10^6\) nodes, \(\hat{A}\) is a \(10^6 \times 10^6\) matrix; impossible to store or multiply with.
GraphSAGE (Hamilton, Ying & Leskovec 2017) introduces neighbourhood sampling: for each node, sample a fixed-size subset of \(S\) neighbours instead of using all of them. This allows mini-batch training:
\[h_v^{(l+1)} = \sigma\!\left(W^{(l)} \left[ h_v^{(l)} \,\|\, \text{AGG}\!\left(\{h_u^{(l)} : u \in \tilde{\mathcal{N}}_S(v)\}\right) \right]\right)\]
(where \(\|\) denotes concatenation and \(\tilde{\mathcal{N}}_S(v)\) is a sampled neighbourhood of size \(S\)).
Because the weight matrices are shared, a trained GraphSAGE model can embed nodes that were not present during training; this is the inductive property.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
section: 6.4 Graph Attention Networks (GAT, Veličković et al. 2018)
text:
6.4 Graph Attention Networks (GAT, Veličković et al. 2018)
Instead of uniform (mean) or fixed-normalisation (GCN) aggregation, GAT learns attention weights \(\alpha_{ij}\) for each edge:
\[\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp\!\left(\text{LeakyReLU}(a^\top [W h_i \| W h_j])\right)}{\sum_{k \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \exp\!\left(\text{LeakyReLU}(a^\top [W h_i \| W h_k])\right)}\]
\[h_i^{\text{new}} = \sigma\!\left(\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \alpha_{ij} W h_j\right)\]
Attention weights are interpretable: a large \(\alpha_{ij}\) means node \(i\) relies heavily on node \(j\) for its prediction.
Multi-head attention: run \(K\) independent attention heads and concatenate (or average) the results. Reduces variance and captures diverse neighbourhood patterns.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
6.5 The Message-Passing Framework
All GNN variants fit a single message passing neural network (MPNN) framework (Gilmer et al. 2017):

Message: \(m_{ij}^{(l)} = \phi\!\left(h_i^{(l)}, h_j^{(l)}, e_{ij}\right)\)
Aggregate: \(a_i^{(l)} = \bigoplus_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} m_{ij}^{(l)}\)
Update: \(h_i^{(l+1)} = \gamma\!\left(h_i^{(l)}, a_i^{(l)}\right)\)

The choice of \(\phi\), \(\bigoplus\), and \(\gamma\) defines the GNN variant:



Model
\(\bigoplus\)
Notes




GCN
Normalised sum
Fixed weights, simple


GraphSAGE
Mean / max
Sampled, inductive


GAT
Attention-weighted sum
Learnable weights


GIN
Sum
Most expressive (WL-equivalent)



GIN (Xu et al. 2019) proves that sum aggregation is necessary for maximum expressiveness: mean and max aggregations can fail to distinguish different graph structures that sum can tell apart.


Code
build_a_hat <- function(A_dense) {
  n       <- nrow(A_dense)
  A_tilde <- A_dense + diag(n)
  d       <- rowSums(A_tilde)
  d_isqrt <- ifelse(d > 0, 1 / sqrt(d), 0)
  D_isqrt <- diag(d_isqrt)
  D_isqrt %*% A_tilde %*% D_isqrt
}

gcn_forward <- function(A_hat, X, W1, W2) {
  # Layer 1: ReLU(A_hat %*% X %*% W1)
  H1 <- A_hat %*% X %*% W1
  H1[H1 < 0] <- 0
  # Layer 2 + softmax
  Z  <- A_hat %*% H1 %*% W2
  Z  <- Z - apply(Z, 1, max)          # numerical stability
  Z  <- exp(Z)
  Z  / rowSums(Z)
}

set.seed(42)
k_gcn <- 3L; n_g <- 30L; n_gcn <- k_gcn * n_g
pref_g <- matrix(0.02, k_gcn, k_gcn); diag(pref_g) <- 0.3
g_gcn  <- sample_sbm(n_gcn, pref = pref_g, block.sizes = rep(n_g, k_gcn))
true_labels_g <- rep(seq_len(k_gcn) - 1L, each = n_g)

A_gcn <- as.matrix(as_adjacency_matrix(g_gcn, sparse = FALSE))
A_hat <- build_a_hat(A_gcn)

# Class-informative node features
means_g <- matrix(rnorm(k_gcn * 8) * 2, nrow = k_gcn)
X_gcn   <- means_g[true_labels_g + 1L, ] + matrix(rnorm(n_gcn * 8), n_gcn, 8)

# Random (untrained) weights
W1_gcn <- matrix(rnorm(8 * 16) * 0.1, 8, 16)
W2_gcn <- matrix(rnorm(16 * k_gcn) * 0.1, 16, k_gcn)

Z_gcn <- gcn_forward(A_hat, X_gcn, W1_gcn, W2_gcn)
cat(sprintf("GCN forward pass (untrained weights):\n"))

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
section: 6.6 Practical Checklist: When to Use What
text:
6.6 Practical Checklist: When to Use What


 Question                   
 Homophilic or heterophilic?
 Transductive or inductive? 
 Node features available?   
 How many GNN layers?       
 Interpretability needed?   
 Scale > 1M nodes?          
 Overlapping communities?   
 Recommendation                                             
 Homophilic → GCN/GAT; Heterophilic → GraphSAGE or H2GCN    
 Transductive → GCN (full graph); Inductive → GraphSAGE     
 Yes: use them as x_v; No: use degree or identity matrix    
 2-3 layers (deeper → over-smoothing, all nodes same vector)
 GAT (attention weights); GNN-Explainer for post-hoc        
 Neighbour sampling (GraphSAGE, ClusterGCN, SIGN)           
 NMF or BigCLAM; hard-partition methods miss overlaps       








ImportantOver-smoothing: the depth problem



With \(L\) GNN layers, each node aggregates information from its \(L\)-hop neighbourhood. For \(L \gg 1\), every node’s representation converges to the same vector (proportional to the dominant eigenvector of \(\hat{A}\)). This over-smoothing destroys the discriminative signal.
In practice: 2 layers suffices for most tasks. If deeper is needed, use skip connections (jumping knowledge networks) or residual connections.

title: Machine Learning with Network Data
text:
7.1 The Three-Problem Framework


 Task                Simplest method                Best ML method           
 Community detection Louvain (modularity)           Leiden / DC-SBM inference
 Node classification Label propagation              GCN / GAT                
 Link prediction     Common neighbours              Katz + GNN (dot product) 
 Node embedding      Spectral (Laplacian eigenmaps) Node2Vec / GraphSAGE     
 Key assumption         Main failure mode
 Modularity > 0         Resolution limit 
 Homophily              Heterophily      
 Triangle closure       Degree bias      
 Proximity ~ similarity Transductive only

title: Problem Set 2
text:
Consider the undirected network \(G\) with nodes \(1, \dots, 8\) and 11 edges given by: \(E = \{(1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (1,5), (1,6), (1,7), (2,4), (3,4), (2,5), (5,6), (5,7)\}\)
Candidate Partitions:

Partition A (structural communities): \(\{1,8\}, \{2,3,4\}, \{5,6,7\}\)



Partition B (degree grouping): \(\{1\}, \{2,3,4,5,6,7,8\}\)



Compute the degree of each node and the total number of edges \(m\).
The degree of each node \(k_i\) is the number of edges incident to node \(i\). We count the occurrences of each node in the edge list above:



Node (\(i\))
Neighbors
Degree (\(k_i\))




1
{2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}
6


2
{1, 4, 5}
3


3
{1, 4}
2


4
{1, 2, 3}
3


5
{1, 2, 6, 7}
4


6
{1, 5}
2


7
{1, 5}
2


8
\(\emptyset\)
0



To find \(m\), we count the unique pairs listed in the edge set \(E\): 1. (1, 2) 2. (1, 3) 3. (1, 4) 4. (1, 5) 5. (1, 6) 6. (1, 7) 7. (2, 4) 8. (3, 4) 9. (2, 5) 10. (5, 6) 11. (5, 7)
Or alternatively following the class slides and using the information that the network is undirected:
\(m = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i = 1}^{n}k_i\)
\(m = \frac{1}{2} (6+3+2+3+4+2+2+0) = 11\)
The total number of edges is \(m = 11\).



For each partition compute the modularity \(Q\):
\[Q=\sum_{c}\left[\frac{m_{c}}{m}-\left(\frac{k_{c}}{2m}\right)^{2}\right]\]
where \(m_{c}\) denotes the number of edges inside community \(c\) and \(k_{c}\) the sum of degrees of nodes in \(c\).
From a) we know that \(m = 11\) and \(2m = 22\)
Community A

Group \(c_1\): \(m_{c_1} = 0\) Because there are no internal edges between 1 and 8. \(k_{c_1} = k_1 + k_8 = 6+0 = 6\) Contribution to Q: \(\frac{0}{11} - (\frac{6}{22})^2 \approx -0.0744\)
Group \(c_2\): \(m_{c_2} = 2\) Because there are only two edges between the group nodes.
\(k_{c_2} = k_2 + k_3 + k_4 = 3+2+3= 8\) Contribution to Q: \(\frac{2}{11} - (\frac{8}{22})^2 \approx 0.0496\)
Group \(c_3\): \(m_{c_3} = 2\) Because there are only two edges between the group nodes. \(k_{c_3} = k_5 + k_6 +k_7= 4+2+2 = 8\) Contribution to Q: \(\frac{2}{11} - (\frac{8}{22})^2 \approx 0.0496\)

\(Q \approx -0.0744+0.0496+0.0496 = 0.0248\)
Community B

Group \(c_1\): \(m_{c_1} = 0\) because there are no self-loops for node 1. \(k_{c_1} = k_1 = 6\) Contribution to Q: \(\frac{0}{11} - (\frac{6}{22})^2 \approx -0.0744\)

Group \(c_2\): \(m_{c_2} = 5\) (Edges: (2,4), (3,4), (2,5), (5,6), (5,7)) \(k_{c_2} = k_2 + k_3 + k_4 + k_5 + k_6 + k_7 + k_8 = 16\) Contribution to Q: \(\frac{5}{11} - (\frac{16}{22})^2 \approx -0.0744\)
\(Q_B \approx -0.1488\)



For each partition compute the maximum-likelihood SBM parameters:
\[\tilde{M}_{uv}=\frac{E_{uv}}{N_{uv}}\]
where \(E_{uv}\) is the number of edges between blocks \(u\) and \(v\) and \(N_{uv}\) the number of possible such edges.
For undirected graphs without self-loops, if \(u=v\), \(N_{uu} = \frac{n_u(n_u-1)}{2}\); if \(u \neq v\), \(N_{uv} = n_u n_v\)
Partition A (Structural Communities)
Blocks: \(1: \{1,8\}\), \(2: \{2,3,4\}\), \(3: \{5,6,7\}\).
Block Sizes: \(n_1=2, n_2=3, n_3=3\).
Block 1 (\(u=1\)): \(E_{11} = 0\) (No edge between 1 and 8). \(N_{11} = \frac{2(1)}{2} = 1\). \(\tilde{M}_{11} = \frac{0}{1} = 0\).
Block 2 (\(u=2\)): \(E_{22} = 2\) (Edges: (2,4), (3,4)). \(N_{22} = \frac{3(2)}{2} = 3\). \(\tilde{M}_{22} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667\).
Block 3 (\(u=3\)): \(E_{33} = 2\) (Edges: (5,6), (5,7)). \(N_{33} = \frac{3(2)}{2} = 3\). \(\tilde{M}_{33} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667\).
Between Blocks 1 & 2: \(E_{12} = 3\) (Edges: (1,2), (1,3), (1,4)). \(N_{12} = 2 \times 3 = 6\). \(\tilde{M}_{12} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5\).
Between Blocks 1 & 3: \(E_{13} = 3\) (Edges: (1,5), (1,6), (1,7)). \(N_{13} = 2 \times 3 = 6\). \(\tilde{M}_{13} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5\).
Between Blocks 2 & 3: \(E_{23} = 1\) (Edge: (2,5)). \(N_{23} = 3 \times 3 = 9\). \(\tilde{M}_{23} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.111\).
Partition B (Degree Grouping)
Blocks: \(1: \{1\}\), \(2: \{2,3,4,5,6,7,8\}\).
Block Sizes: \(n_1=1, n_2=7\).
Block 1 (\(u=1\)):\(E_{11} = 0\).\(N_{11} = \frac{1(0)}{2} = 0\) (Not applicable/Undefined).\(\tilde{M}_{11} = 0\).
Block 2 (\(u=2\)):\(E_{22} = 5\) (Edges: (2,4), (3,4), (2,5), (5,6), (5,7)).\(N_{22} = \frac{7(6)}{2} = 21\).\(\tilde{M}_{22} = \frac{5}{21} \approx 0.238\).

Which partition does modularity prefer? Which does SBM likelihood prefer?
According to modularity, which partition is preferred? According to the SBM likelihood, which partition is preferred?
According to modularity: Partition A
According to the SBM likelihood: Partition B



Explain carefully why the two criteria differ in this example. Your explanation should refer to: * The configuration-model null underlying modularity. * The homogeneity assumptions of the standard SBM. * The role of the high-degree hub.
Modularity compares the observed network to a configuration model null hypothesis, where the expected number of edges between nodes is determined by their degrees. Because node 1 has a very high degree (\(k_1 = 6\)), the null model “expects” it to be connected to almost everyone. When Partition B places node 1 in its own group, it creates a community with zero internal edges, even though the null model expects many; this results in a high penalty that drives the modularity score \(Q\) below zero.
The standard SBM assumes homogeneity within blocks, meaning all nodes in a group must have similar connectivity patterns (stochastic equivalence). Partition A violates this by grouping node 1 (a hub) with node 8 (an isolate). The SBM model finds Partition A less “likely” because it is forced to average the behavior of very different nodes, whereas Partition B isolates the hub, allowing for a much more precise statistical fit for the edges connecting to it. Node 1 acts as a central hub that bridges the structural communities. While modularity focuses on the “assortative” clusters (the triangles in groups {2,3,4} and {5,6,7}), the SBM is more sensitive to the degree heterogeneity. The SBM prefers Partition B because isolating the high-degree hub into its own block provides the most accurate description of the global edge distribution, even if that group lacks internal edges.



Briefly explain how the degree-corrected SBM would change the analysis.
The degree-corrected SBM (DC-SBM) would significantly alter the analysis by relaxing the standard model’s strict requirement that all nodes within a block share the same expected degree. In the standard SBM, nodes are considered stochastically equivalent, which forces the model to prefer Partition B simply to separate the high-degree hub from the low-degree nodes. The DC-SBM introduces an additional parameter for each node that accounts for its individual degree, decoupling the node’s propensity to form edges from its community assignment.
By allowing for such degree heterogeneity, the DC-SBM would no longer penalize Partition A for grouping node 1 and node 8 together. Instead of being forced to isolate the hub to achieve a better statistical fit, the model could assign a high degree parameter to node 1 and a low one to node 8 while keeping them in the same structural block. This shift allows the likelihood calculation to focus on the underlying community structure rather than the sheer number of edges connected to node 1, likely leading the model to prefer the structural communities of Partition A over the degree-based grouping of Partition B.

For each partition compute the modularity \(Q\):
\[Q=\sum_{c}\left[\frac{m_{c}}{m}-\left(\frac{k_{c}}{2m}\right)^{2}\right]\]
where \(m_{c}\) denotes the number of edges inside community \(c\) and \(k_{c}\) the sum of degrees of nodes in \(c\).

Community A

Community B

\(Q_B \approx -0.1488\)

title: Problem Set 2
section: (a) Missing link prediction: Jaccard and Adar-Adamic
text:
(a) Missing link prediction: Jaccard and Adar-Adamic
Imagine we know there is a missing link between node \(B\) and some other node. Using the Jaccard Index, tell me which link(s) is most likely to be missing. Would the answer change if we use the Adar-Adamic index?
The Jaccard coefficient captures the fraction of neighbors of either node that are neighbors of both nodes. More formally,
\[
Jaccard(i,j) = \frac{\mid \ N(v_1) \cap N(v_2) \mid}{\mid \ N(v_1) \cup N(v_2) \mid}
\]
The Adar-Adamic index similarly captures overlap by looking at the inverse degree of shared friends. More formally,
\[
A(i,j) = \sum_{u \in N(i) \cap N(j)} \frac{1}{log \mid N(u) \mid}
\]
Where \(N(v_1)\) represents the neighbors of node \(i\) (edges connecting to other nodes), and \(N(v_2)\) represents the same for some other non-neighboring node \(j\). Looking at the graph provided, using the Jaccard index we see that for Node B it has \(N(B) = \{H, C, D \} = 3\). For all other non-neighboring nodes, \(\{A, E, G, F \}\), we find their number of neighbors as:



Node
Neighbors
Number




A
\(\{E, F, G, H\}\)
4


E
\(\{A, H\}\)
3


G
\(\{A\}\)
1


F
\(\{A\}\)
1



Thus, we can look at simply the intersection and union of these nodes for each \(B-v_2\) pair:
\[
B-A = \frac{\mid \ N(B) \cap N(A) \mid}{\mid \ N(B) \cup N(A) \mid} = \frac{1}{6} \\
B-E = \frac{\mid \ N(B) \cap N(E) \mid}{\mid \ N(B) \cup N(E) \mid} = \frac{1}{4} \\
B-F = \frac{\mid \ N(B) \cap N(F) \mid}{\mid \ N(B) \cup N(F) \mid} = \frac{0}{4}\\
B-G = \frac{\mid \ N(B) \cap N(G) \mid}{\mid \ N(B) \cup N(G) \mid} = \frac{0}{4}
\]
Since \(\frac{1}{4} > \frac{1}{6} > 0\), the Jaccard index predicts \(B-E\) as the missing edge link.
For the Adar-Adamic index, we can simply plug into the formula defined above. Since both connections \(B-A, B-E\) share one common neighbor, \(\{H\}\), we find the index score based on the neighbors of node \(H\):
\[
A(B,A) = \sum_{u \in N(B) \cap N(A)} \frac{1}{log \mid N(H) \mid } = \frac{1}{log \mid 3 \mid} \approx 0.910 \\
A(B,E) = \sum_{u \in N(B) \cap N(E)} \frac{1}{log \mid N(H) \mid } = \frac{1}{log \mid 3 \mid} \approx 0.910
\]
Thus, the Adar-Adamic index gives us a tie between a missing link for either \(B-A\) or \(B-E\). Thus, the answer should not change if we select based on both metrics, which would give us a preference to impute a missing link as \(B-E\).

title: Problem Set 2
text:
(b) Missing attribute prediction: local smoothing
Imagine that a binary node attribute (for instance, gender) is incomplete. Predict the missing attributes (i.e. the white nodes) using the local smoothing algorithm we saw in class.
The local smoothing algorithm predicts a missing binary attribute as the mode (we would use mean as a measure for continuous values) among a node’s neighbors with known attributes. More formally:
\[x_i^* = \text{mode}\left(\{x_{N(i)}^\circ\} - \emptyset\right) \quad \text{if } x \text{ is categorical}\]
Where \(x_{N(i)}^\circ\) represents the set of known attribute values of node \(i\)’s neighbors. Predictions for all unlabeled nodes are made in parallel, meaning we use only the originally observed labels when computing the mode, not the predicted ones.
Looking at the graph, orange nodes have attribute value 1, blue nodes have value 0, and white nodes (unknown) are \(\{H, D\}\). The known orange nodes are \(\{B, C, F, G\}\). The known blue nodes are \(\{A, E\}\). For each white node, we identify its neighbors and take the mode:



Node
Neighbors
Known Labels
Mode




H
\(\{A, E, B\}\)
\(A = 0, B = 1, E = 0\)
0


D
\(\{B, C\}\)
\(B = 1, C = 1\)
1



Thus, the local smoothing algorithm predicts:
\[x_H^* = 0, \quad x_D^* = 1\]
Thus, the two white nodes \(x_D, x_H\) are predicted to be: orange and blue, respectively.

title: Problem Set 2
text:
(b) Remove edges
Remove a fixed percentage (20%) of the edges at random from the generated graph to simulate missing links.

i. How many edges are in the original graph, and how many will you remove?
There are 500 edges in the original graph; we will remove 100 (since we arbitrarily chose 20%). This is because the number of edges can be found as \(m = \frac{n \cdot <k>}{2}\), where \(c \equiv <k>\) is the ‘average degree’ of the network, meaning the average number of connections each node contains. Thus, with \(n=100\) nodes and an average degree of \(c=10\) we get that there are \(m=500\) edges. By removing 20%, we thereby end up with a graph of 400 edges.

# get list of edges from network
list_of_edges = rg.get_edgelist()
len(list_of_edges) # 500 edges

500




ii. What strategy will you use to randomly select the edges to be removed?
The chosen strategy involves calling up the ‘random’ library and generating a random sample, without replacement, of \(m \cdot 0.20 = 100\) items in the range(\(m\)), with each draw being exactly \(20\%\) probability. Afterwards, we using the built in igraph function ‘x.delete_edges()’, of which we feed in our list of random values generated, which correspond to the position in the edge list generated from the Erdos-Renyi graph, and this returns to us an edgelist of length 400.


iii. How will you store or mark these removed edges for later evaluation?
Before applying the ‘x.delete_edges()’ function to our Erdos-Renyi graph, which permanently remove the 100 edges, we can use the ‘x.get_edges()’ function to get a complete list of all our edges between nodes, and store it as an object. Afterwards, we can use simply iterate through the list and extract the elements that correspond to the values randomly generated from our ‘random_edge_list’, successfully storing the 100 edges before removing them.

# remove 20% edges randomly
random_edge_list = random.sample(population = list(range(m)),
                                 k = int(m*0.2))
len(random_edge_list) # 100 edges chosen to be removed

100



# store our deleted edges as a variable before applying
all_edges = rg.get_edgelist()
removed_edges_list = [all_edges[i] for i in random_edge_list]


# apply to our original erdos-renyi graph
rg.delete_edges(random_edge_list)
len(rg.get_edgelist()) # new graph has 400 edges

400




iv. What challenges might arise regarding the connectivity of the graph, and how would you address them?
By removing \(20\%\) of our connections in the graph, there is a non-zero chance that some nodes become isolated with a degree of 0, or perhaps there may arise a clear separation into multiple different components. This would lead to a failure of the Node2Vec algorithm, as it relies on the assumption of random walks through the network. With possible complete edge disconnections, this would likely perform poorly in terms of edge predictions.
A possible solution to this problem would to take multiple different random samples and apply them until we can ensure that the graph is fully connected still; we can check this easily using the ‘x.is_connected()’ function from igraph, which returns a boolean True/False if the graph is connected or not. Once we find a graph that returns “True,” we can proceed without worries.

# test connectivity of graph
rg.is_connected()

True

title: Problem Set 2
text:
(c) Node2Vec embeddings

import networkx as nx
import node2vec as n2v                               # node2vec algo
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier  # random forest algo
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score           # AUC/ROC scores
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity as cos_sim
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc


i. What are the key parameters for your chosen embedding algorithm?
The key parameters for the node2vec.Node2Vec() algorithm include:

dimensions: Size of the output embedding vector per node. Higher dimensions capture more structural nuance but increase memory and training time.
walk_length: Number of nodes visited per random walk. Longer walks capture more global structure.
num_walks: Number of random walks generated per node. More walks give better co-occurrence statistics for the skip-gram model.
workers: Number of parallel CPU threads. Affects only runtime, not the output.
p (return parameter): Controls the probability of immediately backtracking to the previous node.
q (in-out parameter): Controls bias toward nodes farther from the previous node.

High p \(\rightarrow\) more exploratory, DFS-like walks.
Low q → outward/DFS; high q → local/BFS. At p=q=1 the algorithm reduces to DeepWalk.

# build a networkx graph from igraph object by copying edges
rg_nx = nx.Graph()
rg_nx.add_nodes_from([v.index for v in rg.vs])               # add all nodes first
rg_nx.add_edges_from([(e.source, e.target) for e in rg.es])  # then edges

# implement node2vec algo
node2vec_rg = n2v.Node2Vec(
    graph = rg_nx,      # graph object: MUST BE NX GRAPH!
    dimensions=64,      # embedding size
    walk_length=30,     # steps per walk
    num_walks=200,      # walks per node
    workers=4           # parallel threads
)



ii. How does each parameter influence the quality and efficiency of the embeddings?

dimensions: Higher values improve representational capacity but slow training and may overfit on small graphs. 64 is a common default.
walk_length: Longer walks expose the model to more distant neighborhoods, improving global structure capture, but add compute cost with diminishing returns.
num_walks: More walks per node increase the reliability of co-occurrence counts fed to skip-gram. Critical for sparse graphs where any single walk may miss important neighbors.
workers: More workers reduce wall-clock time linearly (up to CPU core count) with no effect on embedding quality.
p and q: These are the most impactful quality parameters. They control the DFS/BFS trade-off: DFS-biased walks learn community membership; BFS-biased walks learn structural roles (e.g., hub vs. leaf). For link prediction, a mild DFS bias tends to work well.



iii. Can you visually represent the nodes in vector space?

from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

# fit the skip-gram model on the walks generated above
model = node2vec_rg.fit(window=10, min_count=1, batch_words=4)

# extract the 64-dim embedding for every node
nodes      = list(rg_nx.nodes())
embeddings = np.array([model.wv[str(n)] for n in nodes])

# project to 2D with PCA so we can plot it
pca    = PCA(n_components=2, random_state=0)
emb_2d = pca.fit_transform(embeddings)
print(f"Variance explained by 2 PCs: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_.sum():.1%}")

Variance explained by 2 PCs: 11.3%

# compute node degree in the training graph (after edge removal) for colouring
degrees = np.array([rg_nx.degree(n) for n in nodes])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
sc = ax.scatter(emb_2d[:, 0], emb_2d[:, 1],
                c=degrees, cmap='viridis', s=40, alpha=0.8)
plt.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, label='Node degree')

ax.set_xlabel(f"PC 1 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[0]:.1%} var)")
ax.set_ylabel(f"PC 2 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[1]:.1%} var)")

title: Problem Set 2
text:
(d) Cosine similarity scoring
For each missing edge, compute a similarity score using cosine similarity between their node embeddings.

i. How is cosine similarity defined mathematically?
Cosine similarity between two embedding vectors \(\mathbf{e}_u\) and \(\mathbf{e}_v\) is:
\[\text{cos}(\mathbf{e}_u, \mathbf{e}_v) = \frac{\mathbf{e}_u \cdot \mathbf{e}_v}{\|\mathbf{e}_u\|\,\|\mathbf{e}_v\|}\]
It measures the cosine of the angle between the two vectors, ranging from \(-1\) (opposite) to \(1\) (identical direction), ignoring differences in magnitude.

# Demonstrate the formula on an actual edge from rg_nx
u, v = removed_edges_list[0]
eu = model.wv[str(u)]
ev = model.wv[str(v)]

dot_product = np.dot(eu, ev)
norm_u      = np.linalg.norm(eu)
norm_v      = np.linalg.norm(ev)
cosine_manual = dot_product / (norm_u * norm_v)

print(f"Edge ({u}, {v}):")

Edge (49, 58):

print(f"  dot product          = {dot_product:.4f}")

  dot product          = 0.6357

print(f"  ||e_u|| * ||e_v||    = {norm_u:.4f} * {norm_v:.4f} = {norm_u*norm_v:.4f}")

  ||e_u|| * ||e_v||    = 1.7488 * 1.5135 = 2.6467

print(f"  cosine similarity    = {cosine_manual:.4f}")

  cosine similarity    = 0.2402



from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity as cos_sim

# positive examples: the 100 removed edges
pos_scores = [
    cos_sim(model.wv[str(u)].reshape(1,-1),
            model.wv[str(v)].reshape(1,-1))[0,0]
    for u, v in removed_edges_list
]

# negative examples: random non-edges of equal size
removed_set = set(map(frozenset, removed_edges_list))
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
all_nodes_arr = list(rg_nx.nodes())
neg_scores = []
while len(neg_scores) < len(pos_scores):
    a, b = rng.choice(all_nodes_arr, size=2, replace=False)
    a, b = int(a), int(b)
    if not rg_nx.has_edge(a, b) and frozenset([a, b]) not in removed_set:
        neg_scores.append(
            cos_sim(model.wv[str(a)].reshape(1,-1),
                    model.wv[str(b)].reshape(1,-1))[0,0]
        )

print(f"Positive (removed edges) — mean: {np.mean(pos_scores):.3f}, std: {np.std(pos_scores):.3f}")

Positive (removed edges) — mean: 0.132, std: 0.092

ii. Why might cosine similarity be an appropriate measure for scoring missing edges?
Node2Vec encodes structural proximity as the direction of a vector, not its magnitude. I.e., nodes that frequently co-occur in random walks end up pointing in similar directions in embedding space. Cosine similarity captures that directional alignment, making it a natural scoring function. Two nodes with similar neighborhoods will have a high cosine score regardless of the size of their own individual embedding vectors.

# Compare cosine similarity (scalar) vs Hadamard product (vector) for the same edge
u, v = removed_edges_list[0]
eu = model.wv[str(u)]
ev = model.wv[str(v)]

hadamard  = eu * ev                                           # element-wise product -> 64-dim vector
cosine    = cos_sim(eu.reshape(1,-1), ev.reshape(1,-1))[0,0]  # single scalar

Hadamard: 64-dim feature vector; needs a classifier (e.g. logistic regression).




iii. Discuss any potential benefits or drawbacks of using cosine similarity compared to other operators
Cosine similarity collapses the pair comparison to a single scalar, which is simple and requires no additional training. The downside is that it discards per-dimension information and assumes symmetry.
Hadamard product (\(\mathbf{e}_u \cdot \mathbf{e}_v\)) produces a full feature vector for each edge, which can then be passed to a logistic regression or gradient boosted model. This can learn which embedding dimensions are predictive of edges and handle asymmetric relationships well. The tradeoff is that it requires labelled training data and a supervised classifier, which can add complexity to the pipeline.

title: Problem Set 2
text:
(e) ROC curve and AUC
Combine the cosine similarity scores from both the positive examples and the negative examples. Construct an ROC curve and compute the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC).

i. What does the ROC curve represent in the context of link prediction?
The ROC curve plots the true positive rate (fraction of real missing edges correctly scored above threshold) against the false positive rate (fraction of non-edges incorrectly scored above threshold) as the classification threshold is swept from high to low. Each point on the curve corresponds to one threshold choice. In link prediction, it shows how well the cosine score separates truly missing edges from node pairs that never connected.


ii. How do you interpret the AUC value with respect to the quality of your predictions?
The AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve) is the probability that the model assigns a higher cosine score to a randomly chosen positive pair (removed edge) than to a randomly chosen negative pair (non-edge). An AUC of 1.0 indicates perfect discrimination; 0.5 indicates no better than random. For our ER graph, we find a AUC value of ~0.525, which is expected for a random probability graph.


iii. What does an AUC value of 0.5 versus a value close to 1.0 indicate about your model’s performance?
An AUC = 0.5 indicates that the model’s scores are indistinguishable from random. In other words, the cosine similarity provides no useful signal for predicting which pairs are edges.
For an AUC close to 1.0, this indicates that the model almost perfectly ranks true missing edges above non-edges, meaning the embeddings have captured the structural patterns that determine edge formation.
For an Erdos-Renyi graph, where edges generate uniformly at random with no community structure, we expect an AUC close to 0.5 since node2vec embeddings have no meaningful pattern to learn from.

from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc

# build labels and scores from pos_scores / neg_scores computed above

y_score = np.array(pos_scores + neg_scores)

fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_true, y_score)
roc_auc = auc(fpr, tpr)

title: Problem Set 2
text:
(f) Analysis and discussion

i. How effective was cosine similarity as a scoring method for predicting missing links?
On an Erdos-Renyi graph, cosine similarity is expected to perform poorly; the AUC should be near 0.5. This is not a failure of the method but rather of the data: ER graphs have no community structure, so nodes connected by an edge are structurally indistinguishable from unconnected pairs. Node2Vec learns embeddings that reflect co-occurrences from random walks; however, with a uniform edge formation there is no pattern to capture. Cosine similarity (and any embedding-based method) works best on graphs with strong communities or degree heterogeneity.


ii. How might different embedding parameters or alternative scoring methods impact the results?
On a structured graph, increasing dimensions and num_walks generally improves AUC. The p and q parameters matter most: DFS-biased walks (q < 1) tend to produce better community-level embeddings for link prediction, while BFS-biased walks (q > 1) capture local structural roles. On an ER graph, tuning these parameters has little effect since there is no underlying structure to exploit. Replacing cosine similarity with Hadamard + logistic regression would also increase AUC on structured graphs by learning which embedding dimensions are predictive, but would not help here for the same reason.

import node2vec as n2v

def run_link_pred(rg_nx, removed_edges_list, removed_set, dims, walk_len, nwalks):
    """Train Node2Vec with given params and return the AUC on rg_nx."""
    m = n2v.Node2Vec(rg_nx, dimensions=dims, walk_length=walk_len,
                     num_walks=nwalks, workers=1, quiet=True)
    wv = m.fit(window=10, min_count=1, batch_words=4).wv

    p = [cos_sim(wv[str(u)].reshape(1,-1), wv[str(v)].reshape(1,-1))[0,0]
         for u, v in removed_edges_list]

    rng2 = np.random.default_rng(0)
    nn = list(rg_nx.nodes())
    n_ = []
    while len(n_) < len(p):
        a, b = int(rng2.choice(nn)), int(rng2.choice(nn))
        if a != b and not rg_nx.has_edge(a,b) and frozenset([a,b]) not in removed_set:
            n_.append(cos_sim(wv[str(a)].reshape(1,-1), wv[str(b)].reshape(1,-1))[0,0])

    yt = np.array([1]*len(p)+[0]*len(n_))
    ys = np.array(p + n_)
    return auc(*roc_curve(yt, ys)[:2])

iii. What are the potential limitations of your current methodology, and what improvements would you suggest?
Limitations: - The Erdos-Renyi graph is a poor test case, as its lack of community structure from random edge generation makes any embedding-based method essentially unlearnable. - Negative sampling is random and does not account for pairs that are structurally similar but disconnected. - We chose an arbitrary 20% edge removal rate; with different chosen values, results may vary significantly, especially if removing edges past a certain threshold disconnects portions of the graph and/or leads to isolated nodes.
Improvements: - Use a graph with known community structure (e.g. karate club) to demonstrate the method’s potential. - Replace random sampling with degree-stratified sampling to avoid disconnections between low-degree nodes. - Use Hadamard features + logistic regression instead of raw cosine similarity for a stronger baseline.
